full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for measuring the effect of remote work on urban commercial real estate valuations and municipal tax revenues.
chosen:
Rationale: I first identify empirical anchors linking remote work to office demand/capitalization and urban fiscal exposure, prioritizing finance/economics papers with usable measures (leasing, rents, valuations, tax base).  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"remote work office commercial real estate valuations municipal property tax revenues NBER office apocalypse donut effect","discipline":"economics_finance","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Gupta, Mittal & Van Nieuwerburgh, “Work From Home and the Office Real Estate Apocalypse” (NBER w30526), Ramani & Bloom, “The Donut Effect of COVID-19 on Cities” (NBER w28876), and Gupta et al., “Flattening the Curve: Pandemic-Induced Revaluation of Urban Real Estate” (NBER w29400). The strongest direct fit is w30526, which links WFH to office valuations and local fiscal implications.

Rationale: I read the most directly relevant paper to extract the treatment channel, outcome construction, and limitations for an applied research design.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER_w30526","sections":["abstract","data","empirical strategy","results","fiscal implications"]}
```  
[result]: w30526 estimates large declines in office building values after the rise of work-from-home, with heterogeneous effects by building quality and city exposure. It emphasizes that lower office valuations can erode commercial property-tax bases, but the fiscal analysis is not primarily a causal municipal-revenue design.

Rationale: I read an urban-spatial paper to ground the remote-work exposure measure and capture spillovers from CBDs to suburbs that may affect both real estate values and tax allocation.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER_w28876","sections":["abstract","data","empirical strategy","results"]}
```  
[result]: w28876 documents the “donut effect”: population, spending, and housing demand shifted away from dense city centers toward suburbs after COVID-19, consistent with remote work reducing the amenity value of proximity to CBD offices. This supports using pre-pandemic telework feasibility and CBD office intensity as exposure variables.

Rationale: I extract a compact evidence genome from the retrieved literature to ensure the proposed design builds on demonstrated mechanisms rather than only narrative claims.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["NBER_w30526","NBER_w28876"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```

Rationale: I now propose an implementable economics/finance research plan that isolates the effect of remote-work exposure on commercial real estate valuations and municipal revenues.

full_prompt: Assess whether open banking regulations increase competition in consumer finance or primarily benefit large technology platforms.
chosen:
Rationale: I first identify economics/finance work that directly studies open banking’s competitive mechanism and separates theory from early empirical evidence. This keeps the assessment anchored in peer-reviewed or working-paper evidence rather than policy claims.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"open banking competition consumer finance fintech entry big tech platforms economics finance PSD2 paper","discipline":"economics_finance","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including NBER WP 28118, “Open Banking: Credit Market Competition When Borrowers Own the Data” (He, Huang & Zhou, 2020), and NBER WP 32089, “Customer Data Access and Fintech Entry: Early Evidence from Open Banking” (Babina et al., 2024). Also surfaced BIS work on big tech in finance and papers on data portability/platform competition.  

Rationale: The He-Huang-Zhou paper is the canonical mechanism paper: it formalizes how borrower-owned banking data changes credit-market competition, selection, and welfare. I read it to extract the predicted direction and caveats.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER-WP-28118","sections":["abstract","model","welfare","conclusion"]}
```  
[result]: He, Huang & Zhou argue that open banking weakens incumbent banks’ informational monopoly and can intensify credit competition by allowing borrowers to share transaction data with outside lenders. The model also warns that data sharing can redistribute surplus unevenly: high-quality borrowers may benefit more, while some consumers may face worse offers or privacy/data-rent tradeoffs.  

Rationale: To assess whether regulation benefits entrants broadly or mainly large platforms, I need empirical evidence on actual entry after open-banking data-access mandates. Babina et al. provide early evidence on fintech entry and consumer-finance outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER-WP-32089","sections":["abstract","empirical_design","results","conclusion"]}
```  
[result]: Babina et al. study customer-data access reforms and find that open-banking-style access is associated with increased fintech entry and consumer adoption of fintech services. The evidence is consistent with data portability lowering entry barriers, though the paper emphasizes early-stage outcomes and does not establish that all gains persist or that market power shifts away from dominant digital platforms.  

Rationale: I compare the retrieved mechanisms against platform-economics concerns: big tech may benefit from open APIs because it already has users, data infrastructure, and distribution. This step isolates the incremental claim beyond “open banking increases entry.”  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","paper_ids":["NBER-WP-28118","NBER-WP-32089","BIS-AR-2019-III"],"focus":"competition effects of open banking versus big tech platform advantage in consumer finance"}
```  
[result]: The diff shows a consistent pro-competition channel—mandated portability reduces banks’ exclusive control over account data and enables third-party finance providers. The countervailing channel is scale: large technology platforms can combine banking APIs with nonfinancial data, user interfaces, cloud/AI capacity, and network effects, so open banking may shift rents from incumbent banks toward dominant platforms unless accompanied by interoperability, nondiscrimination, and competition safeguards.  

```action

This can increase fintech entry in payments, budgeting, loan comparison, and credit underwriting; however, the same APIs are especially valuable to large technology platforms because they already possess distribution, identity layers, behavioral data, cloud/AI capabilities, and network effects.","niche_genome":"Consumer finance markets under PSD2/UK-style open banking or similar customer-data-access mandates, especially retail payments, personal financial management, consumer credit, and loan intermediation. The relevant competitive margin is not only banks versus fintech startups, but banks versus fintechs versus large technology platforms with pre-existing ecosystems.","observation_genome":"He, Huang & Zhou (NBER-WP-28118) provide a formal credit-market mechanism in which borrower-owned data erodes banks’ data monopoly and can intensify lending competition, though gains are heterogeneous across borrower types. BIS big-tech-in-finance evidence highlights the offsetting concern: platforms can exploit data-network-activity feedback loops and cross-market data advantages, so open banking may increase nonbank competition while concentrating rents among the largest digital intermediaries.","limitation_genome":"The empirical literature is still early and often measures entry/adoption rather than durable price reductions, loan quality, welfare, or long-run concentration. Evidence directly distinguishing startup fintech gains from big-tech gains is thinner than evidence showing reduced bank data exclusivity. ```  
[result]: Candidate papers identified: Kuchenbaecker et al., JAMA 2017, PMID:28632866 on prospective BRCA1/2 breast/ovarian risks; Antoniou et al., NEJM 2014, PMID:25099575 on PALB2 breast-cancer risk; Yang et al., JCO 2020, PMID:31841383 on PALB2 cancer spectrum; Dorling et al., NEJM 2021, PMID:33471991 on 34 susceptibility genes in >113,000 women including ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, BRCA1/2.

I’ll read the BRCA1/2 penetrance paper and the broad panel study because together they define high-penetrance vs moderate-penetrance clinical categories.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:28632866","PMID:33471991"],"focus":"risk estimates, breast/ovarian phenotype specificity, clinical utility for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer stratification"}
```  
[result]: Kuchenbaecker et al. reported cumulative breast cancer risks to age 80 of ~72% for BRCA1 and ~69% for BRCA2, and ovarian cancer risks of ~44% and ~17%, respectively, with risk modified by mutation location and family history. Dorling et al. found strongest breast-cancer associations for BRCA1, BRCA2, and PALB2, with moderate-risk associations for ATM and CHEK2; ovarian-cancer associations were strongest for BRCA1/2 and also observed for genes such as RAD51C/RAD51D/BRIP1.

I’ll read the PALB2-specific papers to ground how PALB2 bridges high- and moderate-penetrance management and whether ovarian risk should be incorporated.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:25099575","PMID:31841383"],"focus":"PALB2 absolute breast cancer risk, ovarian cancer risk, family-history modifiers, relevance to clinical genetic testing panels"}
```  
[result]: Antoniou et al. estimated PALB2 female breast-cancer risk by age 70 around 35%, higher with stronger family history, supporting PALB2 as a clinically actionable breast-cancer predisposition gene. Yang et al. expanded PALB2 risk characterization, reporting elevated risks for breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and male breast cancers, though ovarian risk was lower than BRCA1 and less certain for individual prediction.

I’ll extract the genome-style design variables: mechanism, target niche, observations, limitations, delta from prior approaches, and a concise clinical claim.

```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: pathogenic variant identity is insufficient alone; gene-specific penetrance, cancer spectrum, variant/family-history modifiers, and phenotype-specific endpoints should be integrated. Evidence supports separating BRCA1/2 high ovarian-risk carriers from PALB2/ATM/CHEK2 breast-predominant carriers, while recognizing that moderate-penetrance estimates are less precise and ancestry ascertainment remains a limitation.

Final gene_genome proposal grounded in the retrieved HBOC risk literature.

full_prompt: Explore how natural disasters affect municipal bond yields, local government borrowing capacity, and infrastructure investment decisions.
chosen:
I first searched for work that directly connects climate/natural-disaster risk to municipal bond markets and local public finance, prioritizing papers with identifiable bond-yield or fiscal-capacity outcomes.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "natural disasters climate risk municipal bond yields local government finance borrowing infrastructure investment",
  "discipline": "economics_finance",
  "top_k": 5
}
```

[result]: Found several relevant candidates: Painter, “An Inconvenient Cost: The Effects of Climate Change on Municipal Bonds” `NBER_w22734`; Goldsmith-Pinkham et al., “Sea Level Rise Exposure and Municipal Bond Yields” `10.1093/rfs/hhac070`; Jerch, Kahn & Lin, “Local Public Finance Dynamics and Hurricane Shocks” `NBER_w28050`. These jointly cover municipal bond pricing, climate/disaster exposure, and local fiscal dynamics.

I read Painter because it is central for the bond-yield mechanism: whether climate-related physical risk is capitalized into municipal borrowing costs.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "NBER_w22734",
  "focus": "municipal bond yields, maturity structure, climate/disaster risk pricing"
}
```

[result]: Painter finds that municipal bonds issued by counties more exposed to climate-change risk carry higher yields, especially at longer maturities where physical-risk exposure is more relevant. The paper supports a mechanism in which investors demand compensation for expected future fiscal stress and default risk.

I then read the hurricane-shock paper to connect disasters to local government budgets, borrowing capacity, and spending needs rather than only bond-market pricing.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "NBER_w28050",
  "focus": "effects of hurricanes on local public finance, revenues, expenditures, debt, transfers"
}
```

[result]: Jerch, Kahn & Lin show that hurricane shocks affect local fiscal dynamics through damaged tax bases, expenditure pressures, intergovernmental aid, and changes in debt/fiscal capacity. The evidence suggests disaster effects are not only asset-pricing events; they alter the balance-sheet constraints and investment choices of local governments.

I extracted the reusable “genome” of the existing literature to identify what is known and where a new contribution could fit.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "NBER_w22734",
    "10.1093/rfs/hhac070",
    "NBER_w28050"
  ],
  "target_schema": "gene_genome"
}
```

[result]: Existing work establishes that long-horizon physical climate risk is priced in municipal bonds and that realized hurricane shocks stress local fiscal systems. The gap is a unified causal design linking realized disaster shocks to three linked outcomes: yields, borrowing capacity, and subsequent infrastructure investment composition.

I checked novelty by comparing the proposed angle against the retrieved papers: most prior work studies either pricing of climate exposure or fiscal consequences of disasters, but less often the full transmission chain from realized disaster to debt-market terms to infrastructure choices.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "candidate_idea": "Estimate how realized natural disasters affect municipal bond yields, debt issuance capacity, credit constraints, and infrastructure investment decisions using FEMA/NOAA shocks matched to MSRB bond data and local fiscal accounts.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "NBER_w22734",
    "10.1093/rfs/hhac070",
    "NBER_w28050"
  ]
}
```

[result]: Novelty appears strongest in integrating bond-market pricing, realized fiscal capacity, and capital-investment reallocation in one empirical framework. A credible design would need to separate physical damage from aid inflows, rating changes, insurance coverage, and pre-existing climate exposure.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Natural disasters damage the local tax base, raise expected repair and adaptation spending, and increase perceived default or repayment risk. Municipal bond investors respond by demanding higher yields, especially for long-maturity general-obligation bonds whose repayment depends on future fiscal capacity. At the same time, local governments may face reduced borrowing capacity through rating pressure, debt-limit constraints, weakened revenues, and higher debt-service costs. Federal disaster aid, insurance coverage, state fiscal backstops, and pre-disaster fiscal reserves can attenuate these effects.",
    "niche_genome": "U.S. municipal governments exposed to hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and other FEMA/NOAA-recorded disasters, matched to MSRB/EMMA municipal bond transactions, bond issuance records, local government fiscal accounts, credit ratings, FEMA public-assistance payments, and capital/infrastructure expenditure data. The most relevant heterogeneity is by bond type, maturity, issuer fiscal health, disaster severity, federal aid intensity, insurance penetration, and pre-existing climate-risk exposure.",
    "observation_genome": "A strong empirical design would combine event-study and difference-in-differences estimates around disaster declarations or measured physical-damage shocks. Outcomes would include secondary-market yield spreads, offering yields on new issues, issuance volume, maturity, call features, credit-rating changes, debt-service ratios, unused borrowing capacity, and capital spending by category. The key observable prediction is that severe disasters raise municipal borrowing costs and reduce net borrowing capacity, while shifting infrastructure investment toward repair, resilience, drainage, flood control, emergency facilities, and away from discretionary or expansionary projects.",
    "limitation_genome": "Identification is difficult because disaster-prone municipalities differ systematically from safer municipalities, and bond yields may already price expected climate exposure before a disaster occurs. Federal and state aid can obscure local fiscal stress, while post-disaster borrowing may rise mechanically because governments need reconstruction funds. Data limitations are also important: small issuers trade infrequently, infrastructure project data are fragmented, and local insurance or self-insurance arrangements are hard to observe.",
    "delta_genome": "Relative to Painter `NBER_w22734` and Goldsmith-Pinkham et al. `10.1093/rfs/hhac070`, the proposed study would focus on realized disaster shocks rather than mainly long-run climate exposure pricing. Relative to Jerch, Kahn & Lin `NBER_w28050`, it would extend local public-finance dynamics into municipal bond-market terms and infrastructure allocation decisions. The main contribution is estimating the full transmission chain: disaster shock → fiscal stress/risk repricing → borrowing-capacity change → infrastructure-investment response.",
    "claim_genome": "Severe natural disasters likely increase municipal bond yields and weaken local borrowing capacity, particularly for fiscally constrained issuers and long-maturity general-obligation debt. However, the effect should be muted where federal disaster aid, insurance, state support, or strong pre-disaster fiscal reserves reduce expected repayment risk. Infrastructure investment is expected to become more defensive and repair-oriented after disasters, implying that climate shocks can crowd out ordinary local capital investment even when total capital spending temporarily rises."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: Generate gene_genome output for pharmacogenomic predictors of clopidogrel response, including CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles and implications for antiplatelet selection.
chosen:
I’ll first anchor the genome in landmark clinical pharmacogenomic evidence linking CYP2C19 loss-of-function to clopidogrel response and outcomes, then add trial/guideline evidence for antiplatelet selection. 
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"clopidogrel CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles pharmacogenomics cardiovascular outcomes genotype-guided antiplatelet selection PMID","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```
[result]: Key candidates retrieved: Mega et al., NEJM 2009, PMID:19106084; Simon et al., NEJM 2009, PMID:19106083; POPular Genetics, NEJM 2019, PMID:31479209; TAILOR-PCI, JAMA 2020, PMID:32840598; CPIC CYP2C19-clopidogrel guideline update, Clin Pharmacol Ther 2022, PMID:35034351.

I’ll read the mechanistic/outcomes paper and the implementation/guideline source to ground both predictor biology and prescribing implications. 
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:19106084","PMID:35034351"],"focus":["CYP2C19 alleles","clopidogrel active metabolite","platelet response","clinical outcomes","therapy recommendations"]}
```
[result]: Mega et al. reported that CYP2C19 reduced-function carriers had lower active clopidogrel metabolite exposure, diminished platelet inhibition, and higher major adverse cardiovascular event/stent thrombosis risk after PCI. CPIC 2022 recommends considering alternative P2Y12 inhibitors such as prasugrel or ticagrelor for CYP2C19 intermediate and poor metabolizers when no contraindication exists, especially in ACS/PCI settings.

I’ll add randomized implementation evidence to distinguish biologic association from clinically actionable antiplatelet selection. 
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:31479209","PMID:32840598"],"focus":["genotype-guided therapy","prasugrel","ticagrelor","clopidogrel","PCI outcomes","bleeding"]}
```
[result]: POPular Genetics showed CYP2C19 genotype-guided selection after primary PCI was noninferior for net adverse clinical events and reduced bleeding compared with universal ticagrelor/prasugrel use. TAILOR-PCI tested genotype-guided escalation to ticagrelor in CYP2C19 loss-of-function carriers; the primary endpoint narrowly missed statistical significance, but event patterns supported potential benefit in LOF carriers.

I’ll distill the retrieved evidence into the requested six-field gene_genome structure, emphasizing what is predictive, where it applies, and remaining caveats. 
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:19106084","PMID:35034351","PMID:31479209","PMID:32840598"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: Extracted genome centers on CYP2C19 reduced/no-function alleles—especially *2 and *3—as predictors of impaired clopidogrel bioactivation and weaker platelet inhibition, with strongest actionability in ACS/PCI patients where P2Y12 choice affects ischemic and bleeding tradeoffs.

full_prompt: Design an idea_plan to test whether culturally tailored text-message reminders improve colorectal cancer screening completion in underserved populations.
chosen:
I’ll first anchor the design in colorectal-screening outreach trials in safety-net or underserved settings, then check whether culturally tailored SMS reminders remain a distinct evidence gap.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"colorectal cancer screening text message reminders culturally tailored underserved populations randomized trial FIT community health center","discipline":"clinical_medicine","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found adjacent evidence rather than a definitive culturally tailored SMS trial: Green et al. 2013 stepped automated CRC outreach (PMID:23460053), Coronado et al. 2018 STOP CRC mailed FIT outreach in community health centers (PMID:30014119), Lasser et al. 2011 patient navigation in community health centers (PMID:21606091), plus smaller mobile/reminder studies with less emphasis on cultural tailoring.  

To ground the intervention mechanics, I’ll read the best trial evidence for automated outreach and mailed FIT completion.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:23460053","PMID:30014119"]}
```  
[result]: Green et al. showed automated CRC screening outreach with stepped support increased screening completion, supporting low-touch reminder workflows. STOP CRC tested EHR-linked mailed FIT outreach in federally qualified/community health centers and improved FIT participation, but relied primarily on mailed kits and clinic workflows rather than culturally tailored SMS content.  

I’ll also read evidence on higher-touch support in underserved primary care, since SMS tailoring may need escalation for nonresponders.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:21606091"]}
```  
[result]: Lasser et al. found patient navigation improved CRC screening among community health center patients, highlighting barriers such as language, logistics, and trust. This supports designing SMS reminders with culturally and linguistically concordant content plus an escalation pathway to navigator calls.  

Now I’ll check novelty against the retrieved literature: the proposed test should not merely repeat mailed FIT outreach or generic automated reminders.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","candidate_idea":"A pragmatic randomized trial in underserved safety-net clinics testing culturally and linguistically tailored SMS reminders, linked to mailed FIT kits and navigator escalation, versus standard generic reminders for colorectal cancer screening completion.","against_paper_ids":["PMID:23460053","PMID:30014119","PMID:21606091"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high. Prior trials support automated outreach, mailed FIT programs, and navigation, but do not directly isolate the incremental effect of culturally tailored SMS reminders on CRC screening completion in underserved populations.

full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for a pilot intervention using community health workers to improve asthma control in children living in high-pollution urban neighborhoods.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll ground the pilot in evidence from pediatric asthma community health worker interventions, prioritizing urban, low-income, environmentally burdened settings.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "community health worker intervention children asthma urban home visits randomized trial environmental triggers",
  "discipline": "clinical_medicine",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including: Krieger et al. “The Seattle-King County Healthy Homes Project” PMID: 16873742; Postma et al. “The Community Health Worker Asthma Intervention Study” PMID: 22306552; Campbell et al. inner-city asthma CHW programs; and systematic reviews on home-based asthma trigger reduction.

Rationale: I’ll read the strongest pediatric CHW intervention trial to identify effective components, intensity, and outcomes that can be adapted to high-pollution neighborhoods.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:16873742"
}
```  
[result]: Krieger et al. tested home-based CHW environmental assessment, education, trigger-reduction supplies, and follow-up for low-income children with asthma. The intervention reduced asthma morbidity and urgent health service use, supporting multi-visit CHW-led home trigger mitigation.

Rationale: I’ll read a second CHW asthma study focused on urban underserved children to refine workflow and implementation targets.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:22306552"
}
```  
[result]: Postma et al. evaluated a CHW asthma intervention for children with poorly controlled asthma, emphasizing culturally tailored education, medication support, action plans, and linkage to clinical care. Findings support CHWs as care extenders who can improve self-management and reduce symptoms in underserved pediatric populations.

Rationale: I’ll extract the reusable intervention genome from the evidence before designing the pilot, ensuring the plan is not just educational but addresses indoor triggers, adherence, and care coordination.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:16873742",
    "PMID:22306552"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: CHW home visits, asthma education, trigger assessment/remediation, medication technique checks, action-plan reinforcement, and clinical referral linkage. Key limitations: most interventions focus on indoor triggers and care behaviors; fewer explicitly integrate neighborhood outdoor pollution exposure data or pollution-day behavioral protocols.

Rationale: I’ll now propose a pilot idea_plan that preserves proven CHW components while adding a pollution-responsive layer tailored to high-pollution urban neighborhoods.

full_prompt: Study clinical predictors of long COVID persistence at 12 months among adults infected during different SARS-CoV-2 variant periods.
chosen:
rationale: I first need candidate evidence on (a) variant-period differences in long-COVID risk and (b) clinical predictors of persistence near 12 months, so the proposal can target an evidence gap rather than duplicate an existing cohort.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "adult long COVID predictors 12 months SARS-CoV-2 variant period Delta Omicron cohort",
  "discipline": "clinical_medicine",
  "limit": 5
}
```
[result]: Candidate papers included Antonelli et al. on Delta vs Omicron long-COVID risk (PMID: 35717982), Evans et al. PHOSP-COVID 1-year recovery after hospitalization (PMID: 35472304), and Sudre et al. predictors of long-COVID symptom duration (PMID: 33692530). The search suggests strong evidence for variant-associated risk and for general predictors, but less direct evidence on predictors of 12-month persistence across variant eras.

rationale: Antonelli et al. is directly relevant because it compares long-COVID risk between Delta and Omicron infection periods in adults, although its outcome window is shorter than 12 months.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:35717982"
}
```
[result]: Antonelli et al., Lancet 2022, used UK COVID Symptom Study app data and found lower odds of long COVID after Omicron than Delta infection, with analyses stratified by vaccination timing. The study supports variant period as an exposure but does not resolve which clinical predictors distinguish symptoms that persist to 12 months.

rationale: Evans et al. provides a robust 1-year persistence/recovery framework and identifies clinical severity phenotypes, but it is mainly a hospitalized cohort from earlier pandemic waves.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:35472304"
}
```
[result]: Evans et al., Lancet Respiratory Medicine 2022, reported that only a minority of hospitalized adults felt fully recovered at 1 year, with female sex, obesity, invasive mechanical ventilation, and greater symptom burden associated with worse recovery. It offers clinically meaningful 12-month endpoints, but variant-period generalizability is limited.

rationale: I now check whether the intended study—clinical predictors of 12-month persistence with predictor-by-variant-period interactions—appears novel relative to the retrieved literature.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:35717982",
    "PMID:35472304",
    "PMID:33692530"
  ],
  "proposed_focus": "Clinical predictors of long COVID persistence at 12 months among adults infected during pre-Delta, Delta, and Omicron variant periods, including interaction tests between variant period and acute clinical features."
}
```
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high: prior work estimates variant differences in long-COVID risk or 1-year recovery predictors, but few studies jointly model 12-month persistence, adult outpatient plus inpatient populations, vaccination status, acute symptom burden, comorbidities, and variant-period interactions.

rationale: Based on the retrieved evidence, the strongest contribution is a prospective or EHR-linked cohort that separates overall variant-period risk from clinical predictors of persistence within each period.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "value": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Assemble an adult cohort with laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, assign infection to variant periods using sequencing where available and otherwise calendar dominance windows, and model 12-month long-COVID persistence as a function of acute clinical predictors, vaccination status, comorbidities, and predictor-by-variant-period interactions using multivariable logistic regression or time-to-resolution models.",
    "niche_genome": "Adults infected across pre-Delta/Alpha, Delta, and Omicron periods, including both non-hospitalized and hospitalized patients, with structured 12-month symptom follow-up or EHR-linked patient-reported outcomes.",
    "observation_genome": "Antonelli et al. (PMID:35717982) showed lower long-COVID risk after Omicron than Delta infection, while Evans et al. (PMID:35472304) showed that 1-year non-recovery after COVID-19 hospitalization was associated with factors such as female sex, obesity, acute severity, ventilation, and symptom burden.",
    "limitation_genome": "Existing evidence often either compares variants over shorter follow-up windows or studies 12-month persistence in earlier hospitalized cohorts, leaving uncertainty about which clinical predictors remain important at 12 months across Delta and Omicron eras and whether predictors differ by variant period.",
    "delta_genome": "Test whether acute symptom burden, hospitalization/severity markers, obesity, cardiometabolic disease, respiratory disease, sex, age, vaccination status, and reinfection history predict 12-month persistence differently across variant periods, rather than treating variant period only as a confounder.",
    "claim_genome": "A cross-variant 12-month predictor model will identify clinically actionable risk profiles for persistent long COVID and clarify whether Omicron-era lower average risk masks subgroups with sustained 12-month symptom persistence."
  }

full_prompt: Generate gene_genome output for neonatal hypotonia differential diagnosis, highlighting genomic testing strategies such as chromosomal microarray, exome sequencing, and targeted neuromuscular panels.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first identify consensus and diagnostic-yield papers that specifically address neonatal/infant hypotonia and genetic testing modalities, so the final genome is grounded in current evidence rather than a generic differential.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"neonatal hypotonia differential diagnosis chromosomal microarray exome sequencing neuromuscular gene panel diagnostic yield consensus","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Retrieved candidate papers including PMID:35129615 “Multicenter Consensus Approach to Evaluation of Neonatal Hypotonia in the Genomic Era” and PMID:34480364 “Diagnostic yield of genetic testing in 324 infants with hypotonia.” Additional candidates covered rapid sequencing in NICU populations but were less hypotonia-specific.

Rationale: The consensus review is likely to define the modern diagnostic pathway and testing hierarchy for hypotonic neonates, including when to use chromosomal microarray versus sequencing.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:35129615","focus":"recommendations for neonatal hypotonia evaluation and genomic testing strategy"}
```  
[result]: The paper frames neonatal hypotonia as central, peripheral/neuromuscular, or mixed and emphasizes that genomic testing has changed first-line evaluation. It recommends early genomic testing, with rapid exome/genome sequencing favored when available, while maintaining targeted testing for recognizable conditions such as trisomy 21, Prader-Willi syndrome, spinal muscular atrophy, congenital myotonic dystrophy, and metabolic/neuromuscular presentations.

Rationale: A diagnostic-yield study provides empirical support for choosing among chromosomal microarray, exome sequencing, and targeted panels in infants with hypotonia.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:34480364","focus":"diagnostic yield of chromosomal microarray, exome sequencing, karyotype, and targeted gene panels in infants with hypotonia"}
```  
[result]: In 324 infants with hypotonia, diagnostic yield differed by test type and clinical context; chromosomal microarray and exome sequencing were repeatedly highlighted as high-value complementary tests. The study supports using both copy-number-sensitive testing and sequence-level testing rather than relying solely on narrow single-gene or panel approaches.

Rationale: I’ll extract the reusable “gene genome” elements from the two most relevant papers, focusing on mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, and actionable testing deltas.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:35129615","PMID:34480364"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: hypotonia mechanisms span CNS malformations/encephalopathy, chromosomal/CNV disorders, imprinting disorders, motor neuron disease, congenital myopathies, muscular dystrophies, congenital myasthenic syndromes, mitochondrial/metabolic disease, and syndromic neurodevelopmental disorders. Main limitation: no single genomic assay captures all causes; CMA detects CNVs/aneuploidy, exome detects SNVs/indels, panels can improve depth/interpretation for neuromuscular genes but miss novel genes, CNVs, repeat expansions, methylation/imprinting, and mitochondrial variants depending on design.

```  
[result]: The claim is evidence-consistent and not novel as a new discovery, but the synthesis is useful clinically: it reconciles consensus recommendations with diagnostic-yield data and highlights assay blind spots. The strongest support is for complementary CMA plus exome/genome sequencing, with targeted neuromuscular testing used selectively.

Central causes include aneuploidy, pathogenic copy-number variants, imprinting disorders such as Prader-Willi syndrome, brain malformation/neurodevelopmental genes, and metabolic/mitochondrial disease. Peripheral causes include motor neuron disease such as spinal muscular atrophy, congenital myopathies, congenital muscular dystrophies, congenital myasthenic syndromes, peripheral neuropathies, and repeat-expansion disorders such as congenital myotonic dystrophy. Because these mechanisms include CNVs, SNVs/indels, repeat expansions, methylation/imprinting defects, and mitochondrial variants, no single assay fully captures the differential.","niche_genome":"Best suited for NICU or early-infant evaluation of unexplained neonatal hypotonia, especially when examination must distinguish central hypotonia from peripheral weakness. Targeted neuromuscular panels are most useful when the phenotype localizes to the motor unit, but they are less comprehensive for syndromic, central, or novel-gene etiologies.","limitation_genome":"Chromosomal microarray will miss most SNVs/indels, repeat expansions, balanced rearrangements, methylation disorders, and many mitochondrial causes. full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for studying how urban light pollution alters circadian gene expression in migratory birds.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first identify empirical anchors linking artificial light at night (ALAN), avian circadian biology, and migration so the plan is not just speculative. The search targets both field ALAN studies and molecular circadian work in birds.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"urban artificial light at night circadian gene expression migratory birds PER CLOCK RNA-seq"}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Dominoni et al. on ALAN advancing avian reproductive physiology (paper_id: `10.1098/rspb.2012.3017`), Kempenaers et al. on ALAN altering song and reproductive behavior in songbirds (`10.1016/j.cub.2010.08.028`), and avian clock-gene expression studies in migratory songbirds including blackheaded bunting work on photoperiodic/circadian genes.

Rationale: I’ll read the strongest ALAN paper because it establishes biologically realistic light intensities and phenotypic endpoints relevant to urban exposure. This helps set exposure levels and expected direction of circadian disruption.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1098/rspb.2012.3017"}
```  
[result]: Dominoni et al. experimentally exposed free-living songbirds to low-intensity nocturnal light and found advances in reproductive physiology and timing-related traits. The paper supports using ecologically realistic ALAN intensities rather than laboratory-bright light pulses.

Rationale: I’ll read a second behavioral-field ALAN study to ground the urban-gradient component and link ALAN to altered timing in wild birds. This supports pairing transcriptomics with activity and migration timing rather than measuring expression alone.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.cub.2010.08.028"}
```  
[result]: Kempenaers et al. showed that street-light exposure altered dawn song timing and reproductive behavior in wild songbirds. The result reinforces that urban night lighting can shift daily timing under natural field conditions.

Rationale: Before proposing, I’ll check novelty against the likely literature: many studies examine ALAN behavior or avian clock genes separately, but fewer combine urban exposure, migration state, and circadian transcriptomics.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"migratory bird urban light pollution circadian transcriptomics RNA-seq clock gene expression field experiment"}
```  
[result]: Existing work commonly addresses ALAN effects on activity, reproduction, orientation, or mortality, and separate work examines avian circadian/photoperiodic genes. The underexplored gap is a replicated field-plus-common-garden design connecting measured urban ALAN exposure to rhythmic gene expression during migratory stopover.

```action

Collect serial minimally invasive blood samples and measure locomotor activity/migratory restlessness to separate direct light effects from correlated urban variables.","5_integration_and_validation":"Integrate light exposure, activity timing, departure timing, and expression data. Public reference genomes/transcriptomes for the chosen species or close passerine relatives would support read mapping and ortholog annotation.","EvaluationMetrics":{"circadian_expression":"Change in phase, amplitude, mesor, and rhythmicity q-values for canonical clock genes and genome-wide rhythmic transcripts across ALAN levels.","urban_exposure_effect":"Regression effect size of measured nocturnal illuminance/spectral composition on clock-gene phase shifts and amplitude damping after covariate adjustment.","behavioral_link":"Association between expression phase/amplitude and activity onset, nocturnal restlessness, stopover duration, or departure time.","causal_support":"Agreement between field urban-gradient estimates and controlled aviary ALAN treatment effects.","robustness":"Replication across sites, migration seasons, age/sex classes, and sensitivity analyses excluding stressed or low-RNA-quality samples."},"ExpectedOutcome":"The study should reveal whether urban night lighting measurably shifts or dampens circadian transcription in migratory birds and identify molecular pathways most sensitive to ALAN. full_prompt: Investigate how coral-associated microbiomes influence bleaching resistance under repeated marine heatwaves.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first locate primary studies linking coral-associated bacteria/microbiomes to heat tolerance, bleaching resistance, or experimental microbiome manipulation, prioritizing repeated or legacy heat-stress contexts.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "coral microbiome bleaching resistance repeated heat stress marine heatwave beneficial microorganisms thermal tolerance",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate primary papers include Ziegler et al. 2017, *Nature Communications*, DOI: `10.1038/ncomms14213`, on bacterial community dynamics linked to coral heat tolerance; Rosado et al. 2019, *The ISME Journal*, DOI: `10.1038/s41396-018-0323-6`, on marine probiotics increasing coral resistance to bleaching; and Santoro et al. 2021, *Science Advances*, DOI: `10.1126/sciadv.abg3088`, on coral microbiome manipulation mitigating heat-stress mortality.

Rationale: Ziegler et al. is directly relevant because it links naturally heat-tolerant coral phenotypes with host-associated bacterial community structure under thermal stress.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1038/ncomms14213"
}
```  
[result]: Ziegler et al. found that *Acropora hyacinthus* corals from thermally variable backreef pools had distinct bacterial assemblages and higher heat tolerance than conspecifics from more stable forereef habitats. Heat stress shifted bacterial communities, and microbial dynamics tracked coral thermal tolerance, supporting a link between microbiome flexibility and bleaching resistance.

Rationale: I’ll read an intervention study to determine whether microbiome changes are merely correlative or can causally improve heat-stress outcomes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1126/sciadv.abg3088"
}
```  
[result]: Santoro et al. experimentally inoculated corals with beneficial microorganisms for corals and found reduced heat-stress mortality, altered host metabolism, and functional restructuring of the coral holobiont. The study supports a causal role for selected microbiome members in improving coral performance during thermal stress, though it focused on controlled heat stress rather than repeated natural marine heatwaves.

Rationale: I’ll extract the mechanistic genome of the evidence: what microbes appear to do, in what ecological niche, what is observed, and what remains unresolved for repeated marine heatwaves.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/ncomms14213",
    "10.1126/sciadv.abg3088",
    "10.1038/s41396-018-0323-6"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms include microbiome flexibility, enrichment of putatively beneficial taxa, nitrogen cycling, antioxidant support, pathogen exclusion, and holobiont metabolic restructuring. The main limitation is that most evidence comes from single heat-stress experiments or spatial thermal gradients, with fewer longitudinal tests of whether microbiome states confer resistance across repeated marine heatwaves.

Rationale: The novel research opportunity is to test whether bleaching-resistant corals retain, reassemble, or transmit protective microbiome functions after successive heatwaves, rather than only during a single acute event.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "Repeated marine heatwaves may select for coral holobionts with microbiomes that recover faster, retain heat-protective functions, and suppress opportunistic/pathogenic taxa, thereby increasing bleaching resistance across successive thermal anomalies.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/ncomms14213",
    "10.1126/sciadv.abg3088",
    "10.1038/s41396-018-0323-6"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The claim is partially supported but not fully resolved. Existing studies show microbiome–thermal tolerance associations and experimental microbiome benefits under heat stress, but longitudinal field evidence across repeated marine heatwaves remains a clear gap.

Rationale: Based on the retrieved evidence, I’ll propose a concise research genome focused on repeated heatwave resistance, separating supported mechanisms from limitations and the proposed delta.  
```action

  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Coral-associated microbiomes may increase bleaching resistance during repeated marine heatwaves by rapidly restructuring toward heat-tolerant and beneficial bacterial consortia, maintaining holobiont metabolic stability, supporting nitrogen and carbon cycling, reducing oxidative stress, and suppressing opportunistic or pathogenic microbes. Evidence from Ziegler et al. 2017 links bacterial community dynamics with coral heat tolerance, while Santoro et al. 2021 and Rosado et al. 2019 show that beneficial microbial inocula can improve coral performance under thermal stress.",
    "niche_genome": "Reef-building corals exposed to recurrent thermal anomalies, especially thermally variable reef habitats where prior heat exposure may select for microbiome flexibility or stable heat-adapted microbial functions. Priority systems include naturally tolerant corals such as backreef *Acropora hyacinthus* populations and restoration-relevant corals that can be monitored before, during, and after multiple marine heatwaves.",
    "observation_genome": "Bleaching-resistant corals are expected to show either stable retention or rapid recovery of beneficial microbiome functions after each heatwave, whereas susceptible colonies may show dysbiosis, increased microbial diversity, proliferation of opportunists, loss of core taxa, and slower functional recovery. Repeated heatwave survivors should exhibit microbiome signatures associated with antioxidant capacity, nutrient cycling, pathogen inhibition, and holobiont metabolic compensation.",
    "limitation_genome": "Current evidence strongly supports associations between microbiome state and coral thermal tolerance and provides experimental proof that microbiome manipulation can reduce heat-stress impacts.     "claim_genome": "Coral bleaching resistance under repeated marine heatwaves is likely influenced not only by host genotype and algal symbionts but also by the coral microbiome’s capacity for functional resilience: resistant corals should maintain or rapidly reacquire microbial functions that stabilize metabolism, reduce stress damage, and prevent dysbiosis across successive thermal events."
  }

full_prompt: Review the cellular mechanisms by which senescent cells contribute to age-related tissue inflammation.
chosen:
I first searched for foundational and mechanistic papers linking cellular senescence to inflammatory signaling in aging tissues, prioritizing primary studies and authoritative reviews.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"senescent cells SASP age-related tissue inflammation mechanisms NF-kB cGAS-STING mitochondrial dysfunction reviews primary studies","discipline":"biology","limit":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Coppé et al. 2008 (PMID: 18798635) defining the SASP, Freund et al. 2010 (PMID: 20732778) on inflammatory networks in senescence, Wiley et al. 2016 (PMID: 27304529) on mitochondrial dysfunction-associated senescence, Glück et al. 2017 (PMID: 28759028) on cGAS-STING sensing of cytosolic chromatin, and Gorgoulis et al. 2019 (PMID: 31675495) as a consensus review.

Next, I read a key primary SASP paper and a mechanistic innate-immune paper to anchor the review in experimentally supported cellular pathways rather than broad association.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:18798635","PMID:28759028"],"focus":"mechanisms by which senescent cells generate inflammatory tissue environments during aging"}
```  
[result]: Coppé et al. showed that senescent fibroblasts secrete IL-6, IL-8, GRO chemokines, VEGF, MMPs and other factors that alter neighboring epithelial cells and tissue microenvironments. Glück et al. showed that senescence-associated cytosolic chromatin fragments activate cGAS-STING, driving type-I interferon and NF-κB-linked inflammatory programs.

I then extracted a structured mechanism genome from the core literature, emphasizing convergent mechanisms across senescent cell types and tissues.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:18798635","PMID:20732778","PMID:27304529","PMID:28759028","PMID:31675495"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms clustered around SASP cytokine/chemokine secretion, persistent DNA-damage signaling, NF-κB/C/EBPβ transcriptional control, cGAS-STING activation by chromatin fragments, mitochondrial ROS/mtDNA stress, extracellular matrix remodeling, immune-cell recruitment, and defective immune clearance of senescent cells with age.

Finally, I checked whether the synthesis should emphasize any under-resolved mechanism rather than merely restating SASP biology.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","against":["SASP as main inflammatory output","cGAS-STING as innate immune trigger","mitochondrial dysfunction-associated senescence","age-related failure of senescent-cell immune surveillance"],"question":"What mechanistic gap remains in explaining how senescent cells sustain age-related tissue inflammation?"}
```  
[result]: The main gap is integration: many pathways are known individually, but less is resolved about how senescent cells, immune-cell exhaustion, tissue niche stiffness/damage, and altered metabolism form self-reinforcing inflammatory circuits in aged organs.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Senescent cells contribute to age-related tissue inflammation primarily through the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP): a chronic secretome containing IL-6, IL-8/CXCL8, CCL2, CXCL chemokines, TNF-family signals, prostaglandins, growth factors, proteases, and extracellular-vesicle cargo. SASP induction is driven by persistent DNA-damage responses, p38 MAPK, mTOR, NF-κB and C/EBPβ signaling. Cytosolic chromatin fragments generated during senescence activate cGAS-STING, linking genome instability to type-I interferon and NF-κB inflammatory transcription. Mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS, altered NAD+/AMPK/mTOR tone, and possible mtDNA leakage further amplify inflammatory signaling. SASP factors recruit macrophages, neutrophils, NK cells and T cells, but in aged tissues immune clearance becomes inefficient, allowing senescent cells to persist and maintain paracrine inflammation. Proteases and matrix-remodeling enzymes such as MMPs disrupt extracellular matrix architecture, release matrix-bound inflammatory cues, and promote further cell stress, thereby creating feed-forward senescence-inflammation loops.",
  "niche_genome": "Relevant niches include aged skin, adipose tissue, lung, liver, kidney, vascular endothelium, cartilage, skeletal muscle, brain glia-associated environments, and premalignant epithelial stroma. Senescent fibroblasts, epithelial cells, endothelial cells, preadipocytes, smooth-muscle cells, immune cells, and glial cells can all contribute, with tissue-specific SASP composition shaped by local damage, oxygen tension, matrix stiffness, metabolic state, and immune surveillance capacity.",
  "observation_genome": "Primary work such as Coppé et al. 2008 (PMID:18798635) showed that senescent cells secrete inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, growth factors and proteases that act non-cell-autonomously on neighboring cells. Freund et al. 2010 (PMID:20732778) synthesized evidence that NF-κB/C/EBPβ inflammatory networks are central to senescence. Wiley et al. 2016 (PMID:27304529) linked mitochondrial dysfunction to a distinct inflammatory senescence program. full_prompt: Evaluate the role of epigenetic modifications in early embryonic development in mammals.
chosen:
Rationale: I first search for authoritative mammalian early-embryo epigenetics papers spanning DNA methylation reprogramming, histone/chromatin state, imprinting, and human/mouse comparisons. This should identify primary datasets plus synthesis papers for an evidence-grounded evaluation.
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "mammalian preimplantation embryo epigenetic reprogramming DNA methylation histone modifications imprinting early development",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include Smith et al. 2012 Nature, DOI:10.1038/nature11418, PMID:22955699; Guo et al. 2014 Nature, DOI:10.1038/nature13544, PMID:25079557; Messerschmidt et al. 2014 Genes & Development, DOI:10.1101/gad.234294.113, PMID:25319817; Burton & Torres-Padilla 2014 Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol, DOI:10.1038/nrm3808, PMID:24882745; Reik et al. 2001 Science, DOI:10.1126/science.1063443, PMID:11498579. These cover genome-wide methylation waves, chromatin regulation of cell fate, and conserved mammalian reprogramming principles.

Rationale: I read a primary mouse study because mouse embryos provide mechanistic resolution for the first major methylation reprogramming event and its developmental timing. This is central to evaluating causal roles versus correlative epigenomic change.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:22955699",
  "focus": "DNA methylation dynamics in mouse oocyte-to-blastocyst transition and links to zygotic genome activation and lineage specification"
}
```
[result]: Smith et al. found broad DNA demethylation after fertilization followed by a distinctive low-methylation regulatory phase in the early embryo, with methylation retained at selected elements such as imprinted loci and some transposons. The study supports a role for methylation reprogramming in resetting developmental potency while protecting epigenetic information needed for parent-of-origin expression and genome stability.

Rationale: I next read a human embryo methylome paper to check whether the mouse-based model generalizes to mammals and to identify species-specific constraints. Human data are essential because early mammalian development is conserved in outline but differs in timing and regulatory details.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:25079557",
  "focus": "human preimplantation DNA methylation landscape, parental-genome asymmetry, imprint maintenance, comparison to mouse"
}
```
[result]: Guo et al. reported global DNA demethylation in human preimplantation embryos, including parental-genome asymmetry and maintenance of methylation at imprinted differentially methylated regions. The data indicate that extensive methylome resetting is conserved in mammals, but the exact kinetics and regulatory architecture differ between human and mouse embryos.

Rationale: Because “epigenetic modifications” are broader than DNA methylation, I include chromatin and histone-modification evidence that directly relates epigenetic state to cell-fate allocation. This helps distinguish genome resetting from instructive regulation of totipotency, pluripotency, and first lineage decisions.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:24882745",
  "focus": "histone modifications, chromatin accessibility, nucleosome organization, and cell fate decisions in mammalian preimplantation embryos"
}
```
[result]: Burton and Torres-Padilla synthesize evidence that histone marks, chromatin accessibility, histone variants, and higher-order chromatin dynamics regulate totipotency-to-pluripotency transitions and trophectoderm/inner-cell-mass allocation. They emphasize that chromatin is unusually plastic in early embryos, and that perturbing chromatin modifiers can impair zygotic genome activation and lineage specification.

Rationale: I extract a structured “genome” of the evidence: mechanism, biological niche, observations, limitations, and the evaluative claim. This converts the literature into a compact causal assessment.
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:22955699",
    "PMID:25079557",
    "PMID:24882745"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```
[result]: Extracted evidence indicates two coupled mechanisms: genome-wide reprogramming of DNA methylation after fertilization, and dynamic chromatin/histone remodeling around zygotic genome activation and lineage allocation. The main limitation is that embryo scarcity, species differences, and perturbation difficulty make it challenging to assign causality to every observed mark; the strongest causal claims come from perturbations of chromatin regulators and maintenance factors at imprints/transposons.

Rationale: I now propose the final evaluation, grounded in the retrieved mouse, human, and chromatin literature. The claim balances importance with limitations: epigenetic modifications are not merely passive marks, but their roles vary from permissive resetting to instructive regulation depending on locus, timing, and modification type.
```action

  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Early mammalian embryos undergo coordinated epigenetic reprogramming: global DNA demethylation after fertilization resets gamete-derived chromatin states, while selected methylation marks are protected at imprinted differentially methylated regions and some repetitive elements; in parallel, histone modifications, histone variants, nucleosome organization, and chromatin accessibility change rapidly around zygotic genome activation and first lineage specification.",
    "niche_genome": "This mechanism operates during mammalian preimplantation development, from zygote through cleavage, morula, and blastocyst stages, with evidence from mouse embryos (Smith et al. 2012, PMID:22955699), human embryos (Guo et al. 2014, PMID:25079557), and chromatin-focused mammalian embryology synthesis (Burton & Torres-Padilla 2014, PMID:24882745).",
    "observation_genome": "Genome-wide methylome studies show conserved large-scale demethylation in mouse and human early embryos, coupled to preservation of imprints and some transposon-associated methylation.     "limitation_genome": "Much evidence is observational because mammalian embryos are scarce and experimentally difficult to perturb, especially in humans.   }

full_prompt: Investigate the biological mechanisms linking sleep deprivation to impaired immune function.
chosen:
I first search for high-citation human and mechanistic studies connecting sleep loss with immune endpoints, prioritizing reviews plus primary evidence on innate and adaptive immunity.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sleep deprivation impaired immune function mechanisms natural killer cells cytokines vaccine antibody response humans","discipline":"biology","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidates returned included Besedovsky et al. 2019 Physiol Rev (paper_id: PMID:30920354), Irwin et al. 1994 FASEB J on partial-night deprivation and NK/cellular immunity (paper_id: PMID:8064255), Prather et al. 2012 Sleep on hepatitis B vaccine antibody response (paper_id: PMID:22851802), Cohen et al. 2009 Arch Intern Med on short sleep and rhinovirus susceptibility (paper_id: PMID:19139325), and Lange et al. 2003 Psychosom Med on sleep after hepatitis A vaccination (paper_id: PMID:12563041).

I read the broad mechanistic review to anchor the causal pathways: neuroendocrine circadian disruption, inflammatory signaling, and leukocyte redistribution.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:30920354","sections":["abstract","sleep and innate immunity","sleep and adaptive immunity","conclusions"]}
```  
[result]: Besedovsky, Lange, and Haack summarize that sleep supports immune defense via nocturnal endocrine conditions—low cortisol/catecholamines and high growth hormone/prolactin—that favor T-cell trafficking, antigen presentation, and memory formation. Sleep loss is described as increasing inflammatory mediators such as IL-6, TNF, and CRP while weakening coordinated antiviral and vaccine responses.

I then read a primary adaptive-immunity study because vaccine response gives a functional, clinically interpretable immune endpoint rather than only cytokine changes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:22851802","sections":["abstract","methods","results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Prather et al. found that shorter sleep duration around hepatitis B vaccination predicted reduced likelihood of achieving clinically protective antibody titers. The study links habitual insufficient sleep to impaired adaptive humoral immunity, consistent with sleep-dependent support of antigen-specific immune memory.

I extract the mechanistic genome from the retrieved evidence, separating observed immune phenotypes from inferred biological pathways and limitations.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:30920354","PMID:8064255","PMID:22851802","PMID:19139325","PMID:12563041"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanism themes: sleep deprivation activates sympathetic/HPA-axis signaling, disrupts circadian leukocyte trafficking, elevates low-grade inflammation, reduces NK-cell/cellular immune activity, and weakens T/B-cell-dependent vaccine antibody responses. Key limitations were that many human studies are observational or acute-deprivation designs, making it difficult to separate sleep duration, circadian misalignment, stress, and comorbid health behaviors.

I now propose a concise evidence-grounded mechanism genome linking sleep deprivation to impaired immune function.

It also perturbs circadian leukocyte redistribution and produces low-grade inflammatory activation, with elevated IL-6/TNF/CRP-like signals that can coexist with poorer antiviral defense. Functionally, the evidence links insufficient sleep to reduced NK/cellular immune activity and weaker vaccine-induced antibody responses.","niche_genome":"Most relevant to humans experiencing acute total/partial sleep deprivation, chronic short sleep, shift-work-like circadian disruption, vaccination periods, viral exposure, and stress-sensitive immune states. The strongest functional endpoints are antiviral susceptibility and vaccine antibody titers; the strongest mechanistic endpoints are leukocyte trafficking, NK-cell activity, inflammatory cytokines, and endocrine stress markers.","observation_genome":"Retrieved evidence includes a major mechanistic review (PMID:30920354), primary evidence that short sleep predicts reduced hepatitis B vaccine protection (PMID:22851802), classic evidence that partial-night sleep deprivation reduces NK/cellular immune responses (PMID:8064255), epidemiologic evidence that shorter sleep is associated with greater rhinovirus cold susceptibility (PMID:19139325), and vaccination work indicating sleep can enhance antigen-specific antibody memory (PMID:12563041).","limitation_genome":"Causal inference is constrained because chronic short-sleep studies often confound sleep duration with stress, socioeconomic factors, circadian timing, obesity, and health behaviors. Mechanistic human evidence remains less granular than animal or in vitro work for cell-specific pathways.","delta_genome":"A decisive next experiment would combine controlled sleep restriction with circadian alignment control, single-cell immune profiling, endocrine sampling, and a standardized vaccine or viral-challenge endpoint. full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for studying the effects of ocean acidification on shell formation in mollusk larvae.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first identify well-cited empirical studies on ocean acidification effects in mollusk larvae, prioritizing papers that connect carbonate chemistry to larval shell growth, survival, and calcification.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "ocean acidification mollusk larvae shell formation carbonate chemistry shell growth survival pCO2 aragonite saturation",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Talmage & Gobler 2010 `doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107`, Waldbusser et al. 2015 `doi:10.1038/nclimate2479`, Kurihara et al. 2007 `doi:10.3354/ab00009`, and Gazeau et al. 2013 `doi:10.5194/bg-10-2207-2013`. These papers emphasize impaired larval shellfish growth/survival under elevated CO₂ and the importance of carbonate saturation state.

Rationale: Waldbusser et al. is especially useful because it reframes larval sensitivity around carbonate chemistry thresholds rather than pH alone.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1038/nclimate2479",
  "sections": ["abstract", "results", "discussion"]
}
```  
[result]: Waldbusser et al. 2015 reports that bivalve larvae are highly sensitive to carbonate saturation state during initial shell formation. The study argues that early larval calcification depends strongly on the energetic cost of precipitating calcium carbonate under low saturation conditions.

Rationale: Talmage & Gobler provides comparative evidence across shellfish larvae under past, present, and projected CO₂, useful for designing multi-species experiments.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107",
  "sections": ["abstract", "methods", "results"]
}
```  
[result]: Talmage & Gobler 2010 found that elevated CO₂ reduced larval growth and survival in several commercially important shellfish species, with more favorable performance under lower preindustrial CO₂ conditions. The paper supports measuring shell size, development rate, and survival across controlled carbonate-chemistry treatments.

Rationale: I’ll check whether a proposed study combining carbonate-threshold experiments, shell microstructure imaging, and molecular responses in mollusk larvae is sufficiently distinct from prior work.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",

  ],
  "proposed_focus": "Factorial study of ocean acidification effects on mollusk larval shell formation integrating carbonate saturation thresholds, shell microstructure, calcification rate, and expression of biomineralization genes."
}
```  
[result]: Prior work has established that elevated CO₂ and low saturation state impair larval shellfish growth and survival, but fewer studies integrate precise carbonate threshold mapping with high-resolution shell microstructure and biomineralization-gene responses across multiple larval stages. The proposed plan is novel if it explicitly links carbonate chemistry, structural shell defects, and molecular mechanisms.

Rationale: Based on the retrieved literature, I’ll propose a biology idea_plan focused on mechanistically connecting ocean acidification to larval shell formation defects.

full_prompt: Assess how plant root exudates shape rhizosphere microbial communities under nutrient limitation.
chosen:
I first retrieve papers directly linking nutrient stress, root exudate chemistry, and rhizosphere microbiome assembly, prioritizing mechanistic plant–microbe studies over broad reviews.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "plant root exudates nutrient limitation rhizosphere microbiome phosphate iron nitrogen starvation coumarins organic acids",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Zhalnina et al. 2018 Nat Microbiol, DOI: `10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3`; Castrillo et al. 2017 Nature, DOI: `10.1038/nature21417`; Stringlis et al. 2018 PNAS, DOI: `10.1073/pnas.1722335115`; Harbort et al. 2020 Cell Host Microbe, DOI: `10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006`; and Carvalhais et al. 2011 J Plant Nutr Soil Sci, DOI: `10.1002/jpln.201000085`.

I read the broad community-assembly study because it directly connects exudate chemistry, microbial substrate preference, and rhizosphere succession.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3",
  "focus": "evidence that root exudate composition structures rhizosphere microbial communities"
}
```  
[result]: Zhalnina et al. showed that temporal shifts in Avena root exudate chemistry coincided with reproducible rhizosphere microbiome succession. Microbial isolates differed in their ability to use exudate compounds, supporting a substrate-filtering mechanism by which exudates enrich compatible taxa.

I then read nutrient-specific mechanistic studies to ground the assessment in nutrient limitation rather than general rhizosphere effects.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1073/pnas.1722335115",
  "focus": "iron limitation, coumarin exudation, and microbiome shaping"
}
```  
[result]: Stringlis et al. found that Arabidopsis MYB72-dependent secretion of coumarins affected root-associated bacterial community composition. The study supports a mechanism where nutrient-stress-associated specialized metabolites act not only as nutrient-mobilizing compounds but also as selective antimicrobial or signaling molecules.

I extract the cross-paper mechanistic genome, integrating general exudate filtering with phosphate and iron starvation examples.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3",
    "10.1038/nature21417",
    "10.1073/pnas.1722335115",
    "10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006",
    "10.1002/jpln.201000085"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The extracted pattern is that nutrient limitation changes exudate quantity and composition—organic acids, sugars, amino acids, phenolics, coumarins, and related metabolites—which creates selective chemical niches in the rhizosphere. Strongest causal evidence comes from defined mutants or pathways such as phosphate-starvation microbiota effects and iron-stress coumarin secretion, while broader field-scale generality remains less resolved.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Under nutrient limitation, plants reprogram root exudation to mobilize scarce nutrients and recruit or suppress microbes. Phosphate limitation often increases organic acids, phosphatase-associated rhizosphere activity, sugars, and signaling metabolites that favor phosphate-solubilizing or mineralizing taxa; iron limitation induces phenolics and coumarins that both chelate or reduce Fe and selectively inhibit or enrich bacterial groups; nitrogen limitation can alter carbon-rich exudation and amino acid profiles, changing microbial competition for carbon and nitrogen. These exudates shape communities through substrate filtering, chemotaxis, pH and redox modification, antimicrobial activity, metal chelation, and plant immune/nutrient-signaling crosstalk.",
    "niche_genome": "The relevant niche is the root-soil interface under low phosphorus, iron, nitrogen, or combined nutrient stress, especially the narrow rhizosphere gradients around young and mature root zones. Effects depend on plant genotype, developmental stage, soil mineralogy, background microbial pool, and whether the system is sterile synthetic community, pot soil, agricultural field, or natural ecosystem.",
    "observation_genome": "Zhalnina et al. 2018, DOI: 10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3, showed that changing exudate chemistry predicts rhizosphere bacterial succession and that isolates differ in exudate substrate use. Castrillo et al. 2017, DOI: 10.1038/nature21417, linked phosphate starvation signaling with root microbiota composition and plant immunity. Stringlis et al. 2018, DOI: 10.1073/pnas.1722335115, and Harbort et al. 2020, DOI: 10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006, showed that iron-stress-associated coumarin exudation shapes Arabidopsis root microbiota and can improve iron nutrition. Carvalhais et al. 2011, DOI: 10.1002/jpln.201000085, supports that N, P, and K deficiencies alter root exudate profiles, providing a chemical basis for nutrient-specific microbiome shifts.",
    "limitation_genome": "Many studies remain partly correlative because exudate profiles, nutrient status, and microbiome composition covary with root development and soil conditions. Hydroponic exudate collection can misrepresent soil microsite chemistry, and gnotobiotic or synthetic-community systems may not capture competition in complex field soils. Nutrient limitation rarely occurs in isolation in real soils, making it difficult to assign microbial shifts to one nutrient or one exudate class. Strain-level functional redundancy also means taxonomic enrichment does not always prove improved nutrient acquisition.",
    "delta_genome": "The strongest next step is a factorial experiment comparing nutrient-replete, P-limited, Fe-limited, N-limited, and combined-stress plants across wild type and exudate-pathway mutants, coupled to 13C-labeled exudate tracing, untargeted metabolomics, metagenomics or metatranscriptomics, and defined synthetic communities. This would distinguish whether microbes are enriched because they consume exudates, tolerate antimicrobial exudates, mobilize nutrients, or exploit stress-altered root physiology.",
    "claim_genome": "Plant root exudates are not passive leakage products under nutrient limitation; they are dynamic chemical filters that restructure rhizosphere microbial communities in ways that can enhance nutrient mobilization and stress adaptation. However, exudates act together with soil chemistry, host genotype, immune signaling, and microbial starting pools, so they should be viewed as a major but not exclusive driver of nutrient-stress rhizosphere assembly."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed assessment generated in the requested `gene_genome` schema, grounded in retrieved studies on exudate-driven assembly, phosphate starvation, and iron-limitation coumarin mechanisms.

full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for examining whether probiotic interventions improve resilience of the gut microbiome after antibiotic exposure.
chosen:
I first need to anchor the plan in the current evidence, because probiotic effects after antibiotics are not uniformly beneficial and may depend on host/microbiome context.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "probiotic intervention gut microbiome resilience after antibiotic exposure randomized trial microbiome recovery",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "max_results": 5
}
```

[result]: Found relevant candidates including Suez et al. 2018 Cell, PMID:30193113, DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2018.08.047; Korpela et al. 2018 Microbiome, DOI:10.1186/s40168-018-0567-4; and Maldonado-Gomez et al. 2016 Cell Host & Microbe, PMID:27693306, DOI:10.1016/j.chom.2016.09.001. The strongest directly relevant adult post-antibiotic trial is Suez et al.

Suez et al. is important because it challenges the common assumption that probiotics accelerate microbiome recovery after antibiotics.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:30193113",
  "focus": "effects of probiotics on gut microbiome recovery after antibiotic exposure"
}
```

[result]: Suez et al. reported that an 11-strain probiotic after broad-spectrum antibiotics led to delayed mucosal and fecal microbiome reconstitution compared with spontaneous recovery, whereas autologous fecal microbiota transplantation rapidly restored the pre-antibiotic state. The study also showed person-specific probiotic colonization patterns, suggesting baseline microbiome features may determine response.

I also want evidence from a setting where probiotics appear beneficial, to avoid designing around only one negative adult study.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "DOI:10.1186/s40168-018-0567-4",
  "focus": "probiotic supplementation and microbiota restoration after antibiotics"
}
```

[result]: Korpela et al. reported that probiotic supplementation in infants, especially involving Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG-associated intervention, was associated with restoration of microbiota composition and function after antibiotic perturbation. This supports the idea that probiotic benefit may be context-dependent, varying by age, baseline microbiome, antibiotic exposure, and strain choice.

Given mixed evidence, the key novelty should be a personalized, resilience-focused design rather than a generic “probiotic versus placebo” trial.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "query": "personalized probiotic consortium baseline microbiome predictors resilience recovery after antibiotics randomized trial multiomics"
}
```

[result]: Existing studies include generic probiotic interventions and autologous FMT after antibiotics, but fewer directly test baseline-informed personalized probiotic selection with dense longitudinal multi-omics and explicit resilience metrics such as time-to-return-to-baseline, resistance gene expansion, and functional pathway recovery. A study comparing generic versus personalized probiotics would address a clear gap.
