full_prompt: Design a learning-enhanced impedance controller for robotic polishing or sanding where surface geometry and material properties are initially unknown.
chosen:
I’ll first ground the controller in prior work on variable impedance learning and force-controlled robotic surface finishing, then identify the design gap for initially unknown geometry/materials.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"robotic polishing sanding variable impedance control unknown surface geometry material learning force control","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Hogan’s foundational impedance-control paper (`doi:10.1115/1.3140713`), Buchli et al. on learning variable impedance (`doi:10.1109/ICRA.2011.5980558`), Martín-Martín et al. on variable impedance as an RL action space for contact-rich tasks (`doi:10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968476`), and force-control/trajectory-adaptation work for robotic polishing and sanding.
Variable impedance learning is the key mechanism for adapting contact behavior without requiring a precise fixed contact model, so I’ll read the core learning-based impedance work.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/ICRA.2011.5980558"}
```  
[result]: Buchli et al. show that robot skills can be learned with time-varying stiffness/impedance profiles, improving robustness in dynamic interaction tasks. The paper supports learning impedance parameters rather than hand-tuning them, but it does not specifically solve simultaneous unknown surface reconstruction and material-property identification for polishing/sanding.
Because polishing/sanding is contact-rich and the controller must adapt online, I’ll also read a modern formulation where impedance parameters are exposed directly to learning.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968476"}
```  
[result]: Martín-Martín et al. formulate Cartesian variable impedance control as an action space for reinforcement learning in contact-rich manipulation, enabling policies to modulate equilibrium pose and stiffness/damping. The evidence supports learning over impedance parameters for contact tasks, but the paper assumes the task observation/action abstraction is available rather than estimating unknown surface geometry and material-removal/contact properties during finishing. 

I’ll extract the reusable design genes from the read papers to separate established mechanisms from the missing ingredients for initially unknown surfaces/materials.  
```action

```

The remaining gap appears to be joint online surface/material inference coupled to safe impedance learning, so I’ll check whether that combination is already standard in robotic finishing.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"online estimation surface geometry material stiffness removal rate safe variable impedance learning robotic polishing sanding"}
```

The novelty opportunity is the closed-loop coupling of (i) tactile/force-based local geometry mapping, (ii) Bayesian material/contact-removal identification, and (iii) constrained learned impedance updates for unknown workpieces.

full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for optimizing locomotion controllers in bio-inspired snake robots. Encode gait frequency, phase offsets, body stiffness, and terrain-responsive feedback.
chosen:
I first search for empirical and robot-control papers that explicitly connect snake-robot gait parameters to terrain interaction, because the genome must encode frequency, phase, stiffness, and feedback rather than a generic CPG.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"snake robot locomotion controller gait frequency phase offsets stiffness terrain feedback sidewinding sandy slopes CPG","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: `doi:10.1126/science.1255718` Marvi et al., *Science* 2014 on sidewinding robots/sandy slopes; `doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112` Astley et al., *PNAS* 2015 on modulation of orthogonal body waves; `doi:10.1177/0278364913505415` Liljebäck et al., *IJRR* 2014/2015-era survey/control foundations for snake robots; and `doi:10.1163/156855309X431274` Tesch et al., *Advanced Robotics* 2009 on parameterized gaits for modular snake robots.

I read the terrain-interaction paper because it supplies a concrete feedback target: regulate slip/contact and body-wave amplitude on yielding slopes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1126/science.1255718","sections":["abstract","methods","robot experiments","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Marvi et al. show that sidewinding performance on granular inclines improves by increasing body-ground contact length and minimizing slip; their robot experiments vary waveform parameters to match snake-inspired strategies. This supports encoding terrain feedback as slip/contact regulation rather than only open-loop gait tables.

I read the body-wave modulation paper to ground phase/frequency encoding in the two-wave sidewinding model and to avoid inventing unsupported controller structure.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112","sections":["abstract","results","robot model","supplementary control description"]}
```  
[result]: Astley et al. report that sidewinding can be described by superposed vertical and horizontal traveling waves with approximately orthogonal phase structure; modulation of wave amplitudes and timing enables maneuverability. This grounds genome fields for gait frequency, inter-segment phase offsets, and terrain-conditioned amplitude/stiffness adaptation.

I extract a compact mechanism genome from the two most relevant papers, emphasizing what is reusable for controller optimization.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1126/science.1255718","doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112"],"target_schema":"gene_genome","focus":["gait frequency","phase offsets","body stiffness","terrain-responsive feedback","bio-inspired snake robots"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: rhythmic traveling-wave control; horizontal/vertical wave phase coordination for sidewinding; slip-minimizing contact-length adjustment on granular terrain; and sensor-informed modulation of waveform amplitude/phase. Limitations noted: many demonstrations remain terrain-specific and often tune morphology/compliance indirectly rather than optimizing stiffness online.

I check novelty against common snake-robot CPG/gait-table baselines to frame the delta as a combined optimization genome rather than a single new gait.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","against":["doi:10.1163/156855309X431274","doi:10.1177/0278364913505415","doi:10.1126/science.1255718","doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112"],"claim":"Optimize a terrain-adaptive snake robot controller genome jointly encoding gait frequency, phase offsets, body stiffness, and feedback from slip/contact/IMU cues."}
```  
[result]: Partly novel: prior work covers parameterized gaits, CPG/traveling waves, and terrain-specific sidewinding adaptation, but the joint genome explicitly couples frequency, phase, variable stiffness, and closed-loop terrain feedback for optimization across heterogeneous substrates. The strongest delta is multi-objective co-optimization of waveform timing and compliance with sensory slip/contact regulation.

```action

full_prompt: Design a robust trajectory tracking controller for autonomous agricultural robots operating on sloped, uneven, and slippery terrain.
chosen:
Rationale: I first need evidence on (i) agricultural/off-road path tracking and (ii) robustness to slope/slip disturbances, so I search broadly across robotics-control and vehicle-control literature.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"autonomous agricultural vehicle path tracking nonlinear model predictive control slope slip terrain robust MPC","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Backman et al. “Navigation system for agricultural machines: nonlinear model predictive path tracking” (Computers and Electronics in Agriculture, DOI: 10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009), Mayne et al. “Robust model predictive control of constrained linear systems with bounded disturbances” (Automatica, DOI: 10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018), and Rajamani et al. “Algorithms for real-time estimation of individual wheel tire-road friction coefficients” (IEEE/ASME Trans. Mechatronics, DOI: 10.1109/TMECH.2011.2141691).  

Rationale: I read the agricultural NMPC paper to anchor the nominal tracking architecture in a domain-specific controller rather than importing only highway-vehicle assumptions.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009","sections":["abstract","controller design","experiments","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The paper uses nonlinear model predictive path tracking for agricultural machines with constraints and field experiments, showing accurate tracking for slow off-road operation. Its limitation is that robustness to strong slope-induced lateral drift, changing soil friction, and unmodeled wheel slip is not the primary design target.  

Rationale: I read robust MPC theory because sloped, uneven, slippery terrain can be represented as bounded but time-varying disturbances and parametric uncertainty around the nominal vehicle model.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018","sections":["abstract","main results","constraint tightening","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Tube-based robust MPC maintains constraint satisfaction by optimizing a nominal trajectory while an ancillary feedback controller keeps the real system inside a disturbance tube. The theory is strong for bounded disturbances but requires practical disturbance sets and can be conservative if uncertainty bounds are too large.  

Rationale: I extract the transferable design genome from these papers plus tire-friction estimation evidence, focusing on what changes are needed for agricultural slopes and slippery soil.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009","10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018","10.1109/TMECH.2011.2141691"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: nominal NMPC is effective for constrained agricultural path tracking; tube MPC adds robustness to bounded terrain/model disturbances; online friction/slip estimation can adapt constraints and tire-force limits. The gap is a unified slope-aware, slip-adaptive controller validated on uneven agricultural terrain rather than flat or road-like assumptions.  

Rationale: I check novelty against common solutions to ensure the proposal is not merely “use MPC,” but a concrete robust architecture combining terrain-aware modeling, online slip/friction estimation, tube tightening, and fallback safety behavior.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Slope-aware slip-adaptive tube nonlinear MPC for autonomous agricultural robots, using IMU/GNSS terrain estimation, wheel-slip observers, friction-adaptive constraint tightening, and low-level traction allocation for trajectory tracking on uneven slippery fields.","against":["agricultural NMPC path tracking","tube robust MPC","real-time tire-road friction estimation"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high as an integration contribution: each component exists separately, but the combined controller targets agricultural side-slope drift, wheel slip, actuator saturation, and field safety constraints in one deployable tracking stack. Key risk is conservatism and estimator reliability under severe GNSS degradation or deformable soil.

Search also surfaced older SLIP/Raibert hopping control work, but the newer papers better match aerial/terrain-aware control.

rationale (1-2 sentences): I’ll read the two most relevant exemplars: Salto-1P for repeated jumping with explicit energy storage and aerial orientation issues, and quadruped parkour for modern terrain-conditioned jumping/landing policies. full_prompt: Analyze the effectiveness of differential privacy techniques in protecting user data in mobile analytics systems. Include possible attacks, privacy budgets, and utility degradation.
chosen:

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"mobile analytics local differential privacy RAPPOR Apple privacy budget attacks utility degradation","discipline":"computer_science","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidate papers: Erlingsson et al., “RAPPOR: Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response,” CCS 2014, paper_id `arXiv:1407.6981`; Bittau et al., “Prochlo: Strong Privacy for Analytics in the Crowd,” SOSP 2017, paper_id `arXiv:1710.00901`; Tang et al., “Privacy Loss in Apple’s Implementation of Differential Privacy on MacOS 10.12,” arXiv 2017, paper_id `arXiv:1709.02753`; Fanti et al., “Building a RAPPOR with the Unknown,” PETS 2016, paper_id `doi:10.1515/popets-2016-0028`.  

Rationale: RAPPOR is the foundational local-DP telemetry mechanism for client-side analytics, so I read it for mechanism, privacy-budget handling, and the claimed utility envelope. It is directly relevant to mobile-style repeated telemetry where the server is not trusted with raw client records.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1407.6981","sections":["abstract","mechanism","privacy analysis","experiments"]}
```  
[result]: RAPPOR uses randomized response over Bloom-filter encodings, with “permanent randomized response” to protect a client’s stable value across repeated reports and “instantaneous randomized response” for each upload. It reports usable aggregate recovery for large populations but utility degrades for small cohorts, high-cardinality domains, Bloom-filter collisions, and stronger privacy settings.  

Rationale: Apple’s deployment is important because it highlights operational pitfalls: DP claims can be weakened by parameter choices, repeated collection, and unclear cumulative accounting. I read the empirical analysis for concrete attack surfaces and privacy-budget magnitudes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1709.02753","sections":["abstract","measurement methodology","privacy analysis","attacks","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Tang et al. found that Apple’s macOS DP implementation used local randomization but selected relatively high per-record privacy-loss parameters for some data types and collected repeated reports. The critique emphasizes that cumulative privacy loss and longitudinal linkage matter: even if each report is DP, repeated randomized reports can leak more unless budgets are bounded, memoized, sampled, or otherwise accounted for.  

Rationale: I add Prochlo because it represents a stronger systems design: local encoding plus shuffling/secure aggregation to reduce trust in the collector while improving utility over pure local DP. This helps distinguish “DP technique effectiveness” by architecture rather than treating all DP deployments alike.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1710.00901","sections":["abstract","architecture","threat model","evaluation"]}
```  
[result]: Prochlo combines client-side encoding, ESA-style shuffling/mixing, and analyzer-side aggregation so reports are unlinkable before analysis. It targets telemetry analytics where local DP alone is too noisy, arguing that anonymizing/shuffling can improve privacy amplification and utility, though it introduces assumptions about the shuffler/TEE infrastructure and still requires careful accounting.  

Rationale: I extract a compact “genome” from the retrieved evidence so the final claim separates mechanism, threat model, empirical observations, limitations, and the delta over naive analytics.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:1407.6981","arXiv:1709.02753","arXiv:1710.00901"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted consensus: local randomization protects raw client events from an untrusted analytics server, but accuracy depends heavily on cohort size, domain size, event frequency, and ε/δ accounting. Major weaknesses are longitudinal averaging, high cumulative budgets, linkability across reports, side information, and utility loss for rare events; shuffle/aggregation designs mitigate but do not eliminate these issues.  

Rationale: I now synthesize the evidence into the requested schema, explicitly covering attacks, privacy budgets, and utility degradation for mobile analytics systems.  
```action
{
  "tool":"propose",
  "mechanism_genome":"Mobile analytics DP is most commonly implemented as local differential privacy: each device perturbs telemetry before upload using randomized response or encoded variants such as RAPPOR’s Bloom-filter plus permanent/instantaneous randomized response (`arXiv:1407.6981`). Stronger architectures add shuffling or secure aggregation, as in Prochlo (`arXiv:1710.00901`), so the collector receives unlinkable noisy reports rather than raw per-user events.",
  "niche_genome":"The approach is effective for population-level telemetry such as heavy hitters, common settings, emoji/word frequencies, crash-feature prevalence, or configuration statistics when the number of participating devices is large and the questions are fixed in advance. It is less suitable for low-volume mobile cohorts, rare-event detection, individualized behavioral analytics, or high-cardinality exploratory logging.",
  "observation_genome":"Evidence from RAPPOR shows that local DP can recover useful aggregate distributions at scale, but the signal-to-noise ratio falls as privacy is strengthened, domains grow, or cohorts shrink. The Apple privacy-loss analysis (`arXiv:1709.02753`) shows that practical deployments must disclose and bound cumulative privacy budgets, because high per-event ε values and repeated collection can make nominal DP protection much weaker over time. Prochlo demonstrates that adding shuffling/aggregation can improve the privacy-utility tradeoff compared with pure local DP, but only under additional infrastructure and threat-model assumptions.",
  "limitation_genome":"Likely attacks include longitudinal averaging of repeated randomized reports, linkage of multiple telemetry events to the same device, differencing attacks across releases or cohorts, dictionary attacks against encoded strings, poisoning attacks that distort aggregate estimates, and side-information attacks when an adversary already knows likely user attributes. Privacy budgets are the central control: small ε gives stronger plausible deniability but higher noise; large ε improves analytics but may allow confident inference, especially after sequential composition across days, apps, or event types. Utility degradation appears as biased or noisy frequency estimates, failure to detect rare items, larger confidence intervals, need for larger sample sizes, and reduced ability to segment by geography, device model, or app version.",
  "delta_genome":"Compared with raw mobile analytics, DP substantially reduces direct exposure of user records and can make server compromise less damaging. Compared with naive local randomized response, systems such as RAPPOR and Prochlo add memoization, encoding, and shuffling to manage repeated reports and improve aggregate accuracy. The practical delta is not binary privacy, but a tunable tradeoff: robust aggregate telemetry is feasible, while fine-grained personalization-like analytics are intentionally degraded.",
  "claim_genome":"Differential privacy is effective for protecting user data in mobile analytics only when privacy budgets are conservative, cumulative loss is enforced per user/device, reports are unlinkable or shuffled where possible, and analysts accept measurable utility loss. Its strongest use case is large-scale aggregate measurement; its weakest is repeated, high-cardinality, user-linkable telemetry with high ε, where longitudinal and side-information attacks can erode protection despite formal DP claims."
}
```

Search also surfaced several application papers using quantum-inspired genetic/PSO variants for cloud task scheduling, but with less consistent identifiers and heterogeneous benchmarks.  

Rationale: I read the foundational QEA paper to pin down the actual “quantum-inspired” mechanism—probabilistic qubit representation and rotation-gate updates—separate from physical quantum computing claims.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/4235.985692","sections":["abstract","algorithm","complexity","experiments"]}
```  
[result]: Han & Kim model each decision variable as a qubit-like probability amplitude and iteratively sample binary strings, evaluate fitness, and update amplitudes via lookup/rotation rules toward better solutions. The computational burden remains classical and is dominated by population size × iterations × fitness-evaluation cost; benefits are empirical search diversity and compact probabilistic representation, not asymptotic quantum speedup.  

Rationale: I read a representative cloud workflow scheduling paper to identify the target optimization structure and constraints that a quantum-inspired optimizer would have to handle in deployment.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/AINA.2010.31","sections":["problem formulation","algorithm","experiments","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Pandey et al. formulate cloud workflow scheduling over heterogeneous resources with objectives such as makespan/cost under precedence constraints, using PSO as a metaheuristic over task-to-resource mappings. The paper highlights that realistic scheduling fitness evaluation must account for VM performance heterogeneity, data-transfer delays, and workflow dependencies; metaheuristic overhead is acceptable only if schedule quality gains offset runtime.  

Rationale: I extract a compact genome of the retrieved evidence and then check how the proposed contribution differs from simply “use QEA for scheduling.”

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = probabilistic qubit/Q-bit encodings plus rotation updates coupled to classical fitness evaluation; niche = NP-hard heterogeneous cloud workflow/task scheduling with cost, makespan, precedence, and transfer-delay constraints; observation = metaheuristics can improve schedule quality relative to simple heuristics but add iteration overhead; limitation = no guaranteed global optimum and fitness simulation can dominate runtime; delta = deployment needs incremental/online, constraint-aware, bounded-time variants.

```  
[result]: The novelty gap is not “quantum-inspired scheduling” per se, but a deployment-oriented formulation: hybrid dispatch where Q-bit search is used only for medium-horizon batches, seeded by current cloud telemetry and constrained by strict wall-clock optimization budgets. Existing work more often reports offline benchmark improvements than production constraints such as autoscaler interaction, stale telemetry, migration penalties, and scheduler latency budgets.

Therefore, quantum-inspired methods can plausibly improve schedule quality by exploring many task-resource mappings without maintaining very large explicit populations.","limitation_genome":"Computational complexity remains classical: roughly O(P * I * F), where P is sampled population size, I is iterations, and F is the cost of evaluating one schedule. full_prompt: Examine how blockchain-based identity systems can be designed to balance decentralization, usability, and privacy. Identify technical bottlenecks and possible cryptographic solutions.
chosen:
To ground the design space, I first look for survey/SoK-style work plus a representative early architecture tying blockchain to personal data control. This should reveal recurring tensions rather than a single-system bias.
```action

```
[result]: Found candidates including DOI:10.1109/SPW.2015.27 (“Decentralizing Privacy: Using Blockchain to Protect Personal Data”), arXiv:2107.07439 (“A Survey on Self-Sovereign Identity Ecosystem”), and DOI:10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2923512 (“A Survey on Blockchain-Based Identity Management Systems”). Results emphasize SSI, DIDs, verifiable credentials, privacy-preserving authentication, and revocation as central themes.

I read the early architecture paper because it clarifies what blockchains should and should not store for identity: access-control state and auditability rather than raw personal attributes. This is important for separating decentralization from privacy leakage.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"DOI:10.1109/SPW.2015.27","sections":["abstract","architecture","discussion"]}
```
[result]: Zyskind et al. propose using blockchain as a decentralized access-control/audit layer while storing personal data off-chain. The paper’s key lesson is that transparency helps accountability, but public ledgers can leak metadata unless identifiers, permissions, and transactions are carefully minimized or hidden.

Next I read a SSI ecosystem survey to identify system-level bottlenecks: wallet/key management, credential issuance, revocation, interoperability, and governance. These are where decentralization and usability most often conflict.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2107.07439","sections":["identity models","privacy","challenges","future directions"]}
```
[result]: The survey frames SSI around decentralized identifiers, verifiable credentials, user-controlled wallets, and issuer-verifier trust frameworks. It highlights unresolved problems: key loss/recovery, correlation across presentations, scalable revocation, governance of registries/trust anchors, and limited usability for non-expert users.

I extract a genome from the retrieved literature and compare it against adjacent cryptographic identity work to isolate the technical gap. The aim is to turn broad “SSI is hard” observations into designable mechanisms.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1109/SPW.2015.27","arXiv:2107.07439"],"focus":"blockchain identity decentralization usability privacy cryptographic bottlenecks"}

[result]: Extracted mechanisms include graph/cell-based architecture genotypes, mutation over operators and connectivity, crossover between architecture encodings, and multi-objective fitness using accuracy plus hardware cost. Main limitation: much prior NAS targets mobile GPUs/CPUs or proxies, while very small IoT devices require strict SRAM/Flash/operator-validity constraints during the search itself.

Rationale: I checked novelty against the retrieved work and framed the prompt around genetic programming with IoT-validity constraints built directly into genotype creation, crossover, mutation, and fitness.

    "arXiv:1812.00332",
    "arXiv:1807.11626",
    "arXiv:1703.01513"
  ]
}
```

[result]: The idea is grounded in prior evolutionary and hardware-aware NAS, but the distinctive contribution is an explicitly IoT-constrained genetic-programming prompt that treats MCU deployability as a validity condition rather than only a post-hoc objective.

          "Swap subgraphs, then run a repair compiler to fix channel dimensions, remove unsupported operators, and recompute memory layout.",

        "mutation_schedule": "Use higher mutation early for exploration and reduce mutation rate as Pareto front stabilizes. Increase resource-reducing mutations when many candidates violate SRAM/Flash/latency constraints."
      },
      "fitness_evaluation": {

        "evaluation_protocol": [
          "Train each candidate using weight sharing or short proxy training.",
          "Compile candidate through an MCU deployment toolchain.",
          "Estimate or measure latency, SRAM, Flash, and energy on representative IoT hardware.",
          "Fully train only Pareto-front candidates.",
          "Report final Pareto frontier rather than a single model."
        ]
      }
    },
    "niche_genome": "The target niche is neural architecture evolution for tiny IoT and MCU-class devices where deployment constraints are stricter than typical mobile NAS. Unlike platform-aware NAS for phones or GPUs, this setting requires hard feasibility with respect to SRAM, Flash, supported kernels, quantization, and real-time inference. The prompt is suitable for tasks such as keyword spotting, vibration anomaly detection, wearable biosignal classification, environmental sensing, and low-resolution image classification.",
    "observation_genome": "Prior work supports the design choices: NSGA-Net (`arXiv:1810.03522`) demonstrates multi-objective genetic NAS; Genetic CNN (`arXiv:1703.01513`) supports architecture evolution through encoded network structures; MnasNet (`arXiv:1807.11626`) and ProxylessNAS (`arXiv:1812.00332`) show the value of hardware-aware architecture search; MCUNet (`arXiv:2007.10319`) shows that IoT/MCU deployment must account for memory, operator support, and system-level inference constraints.     "limitation_genome": "The main limitation is evaluation cost: measuring latency and energy on real IoT hardware for many evolved candidates can be expensive.     "claim_genome": "A genetic-programming NAS system using typed IoT-aware architecture genomes, resource-safe crossover, deployment-aware mutation, and Pareto fitness over accuracy, latency, energy, SRAM, and Flash can evolve neural networks that are both accurate and directly deployable on resource-constrained IoT devices."
  }
}

[result]: Extracted evidence indicates that current benchmarks mainly reward isolated functional correctness through unit tests or hidden tests; newer benchmarks add temporal contamination controls and real-repository tasks. Persistent gaps include weak oracle quality, limited security checks, poor measurement of readability and maintainability, inadequate modeling of iterative developer workflows, and almost no calibration of when developers should trust or distrust generated code.

  ]
}
```

[result]: The combined benchmark is partially anticipated by EvalPlus for stronger correctness tests, SWE-bench for real-world repository tasks, LiveCodeBench for contamination resistance, and Pearce et al. for security analysis.     "niche_genome": "This targets code-generating AI systems used for function synthesis, bug fixing, repository maintenance, and coding-assistant workflows.     "observation_genome": "Existing benchmarks have exposed important but narrow capabilities. HumanEval and MBPP measure small-program functional correctness using pass@k. EvalPlus shows that passing original tests often overestimates correctness because additional tests reveal hidden failures. SWE-bench moves closer to real software engineering by requiring patches to resolve GitHub issues, but test passing alone still misses whether code is maintainable, secure, minimally invasive, or easy for developers to review. Security studies such as Pearce et al. show that generated code can introduce vulnerabilities even when it appears functionally plausible.",
    "limitation_genome": "Current evaluations are limited by shallow or incomplete test oracles, benchmark contamination, small synthetic tasks, single-language or single-function bias, weak coverage of long-term maintainability, and inadequate security measurement. As a result, a model can score well while producing brittle, insecure, hard-to-maintain, or deceptively convincing code.",
    "delta_genome": "A better benchmark should report a vector of scores rather than a single pass rate: functional correctness, test robustness, security safety, maintainability delta, integration risk, review burden, and trust calibration. Maintainability should compare pre/post patch complexity, readability, modularity, dependency changes, and conformance to project conventions.     "claim_genome": "The central claim is that code-generation benchmarks should evaluate software engineering outcomes, not just puzzle-solving success. This would produce a more realistic ranking of code-generating AI systems than pass@k alone and would better predict deployment risk in real development environments."
  }
}

I’ll read the human-centered debugging/error-message literature first, because the tool must improve novice understanding rather than merely predict a fix.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1145/1753326.1753478","10.1145/3304221.3319754","10.1145/1953163.1953308"]}

[result]: HelpMeOut retrieves community debugging suggestions from previously seen error contexts, showing value in contextual examples but relying on matching past cases. Becker et al. synthesize evidence that standard compiler/error messages often fail novices due to jargon, poor localization, and lack of actionable guidance. Marceau et al. show that even “improved” messages can have uneven effects, emphasizing the need to evaluate novice comprehension and behavior, not just message quality.

{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:1609.02433","10.1145/3377811.3380343"]}

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1145/1753326.1753478","10.1145/3304221.3319754","10.1145/1953163.1953308","arXiv:1609.02433","10.1145/3377811.3380343"]}

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = retrieval/community examples plus neural localization/repair; niche = CS1/novice programming environments; observation = novices need localized, actionable, jargon-light feedback; limitation = many systems target syntax/compile errors or direct fixes, with limited support for runtime-state explanation; delta = combine runtime tracing, ML diagnosis, and pedagogical natural-language explanation evaluated with learners.

I’ll run a novelty check on a proposed project that explains runtime errors from traces, because the main risk is duplicating automated repair or enhanced compiler-message systems.

full_prompt: Create a study on adaptive user interfaces that personalize accessibility features for users with motor or visual impairments. Address ethics, evaluation, and inclusive design.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first ground the study in established work on adaptive accessibility interfaces, especially systems that personalize UIs for motor and visual impairments.  
```action

  "tool": "search",
  "query": "adaptive user interfaces accessibility motor impairments visual impairments personalized interface study",
  "discipline": "computer_science",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates:  
1. `doi:10.1145/1753326.1753439` — Gajos, Weld, and Wobbrock, “Automatically generating personalized user interfaces with Supple,” CHI 2010.  
2. `doi:10.1145/1952383.1952384` — Wobbrock et al., “Ability-Based Design,” ACM TOCHI 2011.  
3. `doi:10.1145/1414471.1414487` — Kane et al., “Slide Rule: Making mobile touch screens accessible to blind people,” ASSETS 2008.  
4. `doi:10.1145/2702123.2702296` — Azenkot and Lee, work on touchscreen accessibility for blind users, CHI-related accessibility research.  

Rationale: Supple is directly relevant because it personalizes interfaces for users with motor and visual impairments using user-performance models.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1145/1753326.1753439"
}
```  
[result]: The paper presents Supple, a system that automatically generates personalized interfaces by modeling user abilities and optimizing UI layouts. It reports performance benefits for users with motor and vision impairments, but its personalization is mostly task- and model-driven rather than continuously adaptive in real-world longitudinal use.

Rationale: Ability-Based Design provides the inclusive-design framing: systems should adapt to users’ abilities rather than requiring users to adapt to fixed interfaces.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1145/1952383.1952384"
}
```  
[result]: Ability-Based Design argues that interfaces should sense, model, and adapt to users’ capabilities while preserving agency and avoiding deficit-centered framing. It emphasizes adaptation, transparency, and matching interaction demands to user abilities, but leaves open how to ethically evaluate adaptive systems in deployed contexts.

Rationale: Before proposing the study, I’ll check novelty against the retrieved literature: the gap appears to be ethical, longitudinal, user-controlled adaptation across both motor and visual accessibility features.

full_prompt: Study how neuromorphic computing hardware can accelerate spiking neural networks for real-time sensory processing. Compare efficiency against GPU and TPU baselines.
chosen:
I will ground the comparison in neuromorphic chips with published sensory-processing benchmarks, then identify a concrete experimental plan for GPU/TPU baselines. The key is to separate *event-driven sparse inference energy* from raw throughput, since GPUs/TPUs can win at dense batching while neuromorphic systems target low-latency streaming.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"neuromorphic hardware spiking neural network real-time sensory processing energy efficiency GPU TPU Loihi keyword spotting benchmark","discipline":"computer_science","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Davies et al. 2018 on Intel Loihi (DOI:10.1109/MM.2018.112130359), Blouw et al. 2019 keyword spotting on Loihi (arXiv:1812.01739), Merolla et al. 2014 IBM TrueNorth (DOI:10.1126/science.1254642), and NeuroBench-style benchmarking papers for standardized neuromorphic evaluation.

To capture the hardware mechanism, I read the Loihi architecture paper because it explains why SNNs can be efficient: asynchronous spike routing, local memory/compute, and on-chip learning. This provides the causal basis for comparing against GPU/TPU SIMD/systolic dense execution.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"DOI:10.1109/MM.2018.112130359","sections":["abstract","architecture","evaluation"]}
```  
[result]: Davies et al. describe Loihi as a manycore neuromorphic processor with asynchronous spiking communication, distributed SRAM near neuron/synapse compute, programmable synaptic learning, and event-driven execution. The paper reports large energy-delay advantages on sparse, temporally structured workloads, but does not itself provide a full GPU/TPU sensory benchmark suite.

Next I read a task-level benchmark because keyword spotting is a real-time sensory workload with direct conventional-hardware comparisons. This is stronger evidence than architecture-only claims.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1812.01739","sections":["abstract","methods","results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Blouw et al. benchmark a spiking keyword-spotting model on Loihi against conventional CPU/GPU/embedded accelerators and report substantially lower energy per inference for Loihi under streaming inference. The result supports neuromorphic advantage when inputs are sparse, temporal, and processed at low batch sizes, while noting that accuracy/model conversion and workload choice affect the comparison.

I then extract the design genome from the evidence and check novelty against existing benchmarking work. The gap is not “neuromorphic is efficient” in general, but a controlled GPU/TPU comparison across batching, sparsity, latency, and sensor modality.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1109/MM.2018.112130359","arXiv:1812.01739","DOI:10.1126/science.1254642"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = event-driven spike computation with local memory and asynchronous routing; niche = streaming sensory workloads such as audio keyword spotting and event-camera vision; observation = energy/latency benefits appear strongest at low batch size and sparse activity; limitation = comparisons are often chip/task-specific and not normalized against modern GPU/TPU deployments; delta = need standardized SNN-vs-ANN baselines under equal accuracy and latency constraints; claim = neuromorphic hardware can outperform dense accelerators in energy per decision for sparse real-time streams.

Before proposing, I validate that the contribution is not just another hardware benchmark but a modality- and operating-regime map: when Loihi-like neuromorphic systems beat GPU/TPU and when they do not.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Benchmark neuromorphic SNN acceleration for real-time sensory processing against GPU and TPU baselines across event-camera vision and keyword spotting, sweeping sparsity, batch size, latency deadline, and accuracy-matched ANN/SNN models.","corpus":["arXiv:1812.01739","DOI:10.1109/MM.2018.112130359","DOI:10.1126/science.1254642"],"k":5}
```  
[result]: Related work benchmarks Loihi or TrueNorth on selected tasks, especially keyword spotting, and reports energy advantages over conventional processors. The less-covered novelty is a controlled cross-hardware phase diagram including modern GPU and TPU baselines, identical latency constraints, equalized accuracy, and explicit sparsity/batch-size sweeps.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","proposal":{"mechanism_genome":"Neuromorphic hardware accelerates SNNs by exploiting event-driven sparse computation: neurons and synapses update only on spikes, memory is colocated with compute, and spike routing is asynchronous rather than dense matrix-oriented. This contrasts with GPU/TPU execution, which is optimized for dense SIMD/systolic tensor operations and typically relies on batching to amortize overhead.","niche_genome":"The strongest target niche is real-time sensory processing with low batch size, strict latency, and sparse temporal structure: keyword spotting, always-on audio, event-camera gesture/object recognition, tactile sensing, and low-power edge perception. These settings match Loihi/TrueNorth-style hardware better than dense high-throughput GPU/TPU inference.","observation_genome":"Evidence from Loihi architecture work (Davies et al., DOI:10.1109/MM.2018.112130359) and keyword-spotting benchmarking (Blouw et al., arXiv:1812.01739) indicates large energy-per-inference advantages for neuromorphic execution on sparse streaming SNN workloads. Prior results suggest neuromorphic systems are most favorable at batch size 1, under real-time deadlines, and when spike activity remains sparse; GPUs/TPUs become more competitive when workloads are dense, batched, or converted to conventional ANN inference.","limitation_genome":"Existing comparisons are often task-specific, use different model architectures or accuracy points, and rarely include modern GPU and TPU baselines under identical latency, accuracy, and power-measurement protocols. TPU comparison is especially under-characterized because TPUs excel at dense matrix workloads, whereas SNNs require either sparse/event-driven support or ANN-equivalent baselines.","delta_genome":"Run an accuracy-matched benchmark suite comparing neuromorphic SNNs against GPU and TPU baselines across two sensory modalities: audio keyword spotting and event-camera vision. Sweep spike sparsity, batch size, sequence length, and latency deadline; report energy per correct inference, end-to-end latency, throughput, accuracy, and power. Include both native SNNs on neuromorphic hardware and ANN/SNN-equivalent models on GPU/TPU to separate algorithmic from hardware effects.","claim_genome":"Neuromorphic hardware should outperform GPU/TPU baselines in energy efficiency for sparse, low-latency, batch-1 sensory streams, while GPU/TPU systems should remain superior for dense, batched, high-throughput inference. The expected contribution is not a universal winner claim, but a regime map showing where neuromorphic SNN acceleration is practically advantageous for real-time sensory processing."}}
```

These provide evidence for search-based test generation, drone perception-control models, and safety-violation fitness design.

I read the search-based testing paper to extract reusable evolutionary-testing structure: feature-space exploration, behavioral diversity, and failure-oriented objective functions.

[result]: DeepHyperion uses illumination search to generate diverse, failure-revealing inputs for DNN-based systems, emphasizing coverage of behaviorally meaningful feature maps rather than only maximizing crashes. This supports encoding adversarial drone scenarios as evolvable genomes and scoring them with both safety violation and diversity/coverage metrics.

I then read a drone-navigation paper to ground the project in realistic UAV perception-control loops and operational variables.

```action

  "tool": "read",

  "focus": [

[result]: DroNet demonstrates vision-based drone navigation using camera input to estimate steering and collision probability, showing that UAV autonomy is sensitive to visual scene structure, obstacles, and control latency. This motivates adversarial test genomes that alter obstacle geometry, visual conditions, wind, timing delays, and moving-agent behavior.

I compare the retrieved mechanisms against the requested research goal to identify the project’s delta: extending autonomous-driving test evolution into 3D drone-specific adversarial environments with explicit safety fitness.

[result]: Existing work supports search-based adversarial scenario generation and drone visual navigation, but less directly encodes full 3D UAV-specific scenario genomes with wind fields, obstacle dynamics, geofences, sensor faults, and mission-level safety metrics. The strongest novelty is a structured, evolvable drone-scenario genome evaluated in simulation by safety-critical trajectory outcomes.

Key niche parameters are airspace size, obstacle density, vertical clearance, moving-agent density, wind shear, lighting condition, texture ambiguity, GPS availability, communication bandwidth, weather severity, and mission criticality. The genome should explicitly support niche stratification so that the evolutionary search does not only find trivial crashes, but discovers diverse failure modes across low-visibility flight, narrow-gap traversal, dynamic-obstacle avoidance, GPS-denied navigation, landing under crosswind, and return-to-home under degraded sensing.",
    "observation_genome": "Each evolved test case is executed in a high-fidelity simulator such as AirSim, Gazebo, or Isaac Sim connected to the target drone navigation stack. Observations collected per rollout include full drone trajectory, minimum distance to obstacles, geofence violations, collision events, near-miss events, control saturation, attitude instability, localization error, waypoint deviation, mission completion status, emergency-stop activation, recovery behavior, and perception-confidence traces. Safety fitness metrics include collision indicator, minimum time-to-collision, minimum obstacle clearance, geofence breach magnitude, altitude floor/ceiling violation, path deviation from mission corridor, jerk and angular-rate exceedance, control saturation duration, localization divergence, unsafe landing score, and recovery failure after perturbation. A secondary diversity score measures novelty in scenario features and failure signatures, following the motivation of illumination-style search in prior autonomous-system testing work such as arXiv:2006.06435.",
    "limitation_genome": "The main limitation is the simulation-to-real gap: evolved adversarial cases may exploit simulator artifacts rather than genuine drone-navigation weaknesses. Fitness functions can also overemphasize obvious crashes unless constrained by realism and diversity objectives. High-fidelity simulation is computationally expensive, especially when evolving dynamic obstacles, wind fields, and sensor faults jointly. Another limitation is safety validation: real-world execution of evolved cases requires strict containment, hardware-in-the-loop staging, and human override. The project must therefore include plausibility constraints, domain-randomized validation, and staged transfer from pure simulation to software-in-the-loop, hardware-in-the-loop, and controlled physical tests.",
    "delta_genome": "Compared with prior autonomous-driving adversarial test generation, this project’s delta is a drone-specific, 3D, mission-aware genome that jointly evolves environment geometry, aerial dynamics, wind, perception corruption, communication delay, and moving obstacles. Compared with drone-navigation models such as arXiv:1803.08876, the project does not propose a new controller; instead, it proposes a systematic adversarial test generator for exposing safety-critical weaknesses in existing controllers. Compared with generic fuzzing approaches, the genome is semantically structured, physically repaired, and scored with multi-objective safety, realism, and diversity metrics.",
    "claim_genome": "A structured evolutionary scenario genome can discover more diverse and safety-relevant adversarial drone-navigation failures than random scenario sampling or hand-authored stress tests. The expected claim is that multi-objective evolution over drone-specific environmental, sensor, dynamic, and mission genes will increase coverage of rare but plausible failure modes, including near-miss trajectories, geofence breaches, unsafe emergency landings, localization-induced route divergence, and wind-assisted obstacle collisions. The project should report improvements in failure discovery rate, behavioral diversity, minimum safety margin reduction, and reproducibility of discovered test cases across simulator seeds and navigation-stack variants."

}

full_prompt: Examine how multimodal AI systems can be evaluated for grounded reasoning across text, image, audio, and video inputs. Propose benchmark tasks and failure taxonomies.
chosen:
I’ll first identify recent multimodal evaluation papers that cover image, video, and audio, then extract what they measure and where grounding gaps remain. This establishes an evidence base before proposing benchmark tasks and a failure taxonomy.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"multimodal AI benchmark grounded reasoning text image audio video MME MMBench SEED-Bench Video-MME AudioBench arXiv","discipline":"computer_science","max_results":6}
```
[result]: Found relevant benchmarks: MME (`arXiv:2306.13394`), MMBench (`arXiv:2307.06281`), SEED-Bench (`arXiv:2307.16125`), MMMU (`arXiv:2311.16502`), Video-MME (`arXiv:2405.21075`), and AudioBench (`arXiv:2406.16020`). The candidates collectively cover perception/reasoning for images, domain knowledge, video understanding, and audio-language models, but most evaluate answer accuracy more than explicit evidence grounding.

I’ll read representative benchmarks spanning image-centric, video-centric, and audio-centric evaluation to ground the proposal in concrete limitations and task formats.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:2307.06281","arXiv:2405.21075","arXiv:2406.16020"],"sections":["abstract","benchmark design","evaluation","limitations"]}
```
[result]: MMBench uses carefully designed multiple-choice questions and circular evaluation to assess multimodal LLMs on image understanding and reasoning. Video-MME evaluates multi-duration video understanding with subtitles across perception, cognition, and commonsense, exposing long-context temporal failures. AudioBench evaluates audio-language models across speech, sound, and music tasks, but grounding is often implicit in the final answer rather than verified via cited temporal/audio evidence.

Next I’ll extract the “genome” of the existing work: mechanisms used, niches covered, observations, and limitations, so the proposed benchmark extends rather than duplicates them.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:2306.13394","arXiv:2307.06281","arXiv:2307.16125","arXiv:2405.21075","arXiv:2406.16020"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: Existing mechanisms include curated multiple-choice QA, perception/reasoning splits, instruction-style prompting, video duration stratification, subtitle/no-subtitle variants, and audio category stratification. Niches include image perception/reasoning, video temporal understanding, and audio-language comprehension; the main limitation is weak verification that an answer is grounded in the correct visual region, audio segment, video interval, or cross-modal evidence chain.

I’ll check novelty of a benchmark centered on explicit evidence-grounded reasoning across all four modalities, with perturbation-based validation and a shared failure taxonomy.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"A multimodal grounded reasoning benchmark requiring answers plus modality-specific evidence: text spans, image regions, audio timestamps/sources, and video temporal segments; includes counterfactual and modality-ablation tests plus a unified failure taxonomy across text, image, audio, and video.","against_paper_ids":["arXiv:2306.13394","arXiv:2307.06281","arXiv:2307.16125","arXiv:2311.16502","arXiv:2405.21075","arXiv:2406.16020"]}
```
[result]: Novelty appears moderate-to-high. Prior benchmarks evaluate multimodal answer correctness and some temporal/video or audio understanding, but do not systematically require aligned evidence artifacts across text spans, image boxes/masks, audio timestamps, and video intervals, nor do they use unified perturbation tests to separate correct reasoning from language priors or spurious modality use.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Create a benchmark, tentatively GroundMM-Reason, where every item contains a multimodal input bundle drawn from text, image, audio, and video plus a question requiring grounded reasoning. Each model must output: (1) an answer, (2) a compact reasoning trace, and (3) evidence pointers in modality-native form: text spans, image boxes or masks, audio timestamps/source labels, and video frame/time intervals. Core task families should include: cross-modal entity linking, temporal event ordering, audio-visual causality, text-image contradiction detection, video-with-subtitle disambiguation, multimodal counting/state tracking, and counterfactual grounding where one modality is edited while others remain constant.","niche_genome":"This targets evaluation gaps left by MME arXiv:2306.13394, MMBench arXiv:2307.06281, SEED-Bench arXiv:2307.16125, Video-MME arXiv:2405.21075, and AudioBench arXiv:2406.16020: they cover many multimodal skills, but grounding is often inferred from answer correctness. The niche is four-modality, evidence-verifiable reasoning where correctness depends on using the right modality at the right location/time rather than exploiting priors or captions alone.","observation_genome":"Observed benchmark patterns suggest that image benchmarks emphasize perception/reasoning splits, video benchmarks reveal long-context temporal failures, and audio benchmarks expose speech/sound/music comprehension issues. Therefore, GroundMM-Reason should report both answer-level and evidence-level scores: answer accuracy/F1, evidence IoU for image regions, temporal IoU for audio/video segments, text-span F1, cross-modal consistency, calibration under missing/noisy modalities, and robustness under counterfactual edits.","limitation_genome":"The main risks are annotation cost, ambiguous evidence boundaries, and the possibility that free-form rationales are fluent but unfaithful. To reduce this, benchmark items should be built from source media with independently annotatable evidence, include multiple valid evidence sets where appropriate, use constrained evidence formats, and evaluate rationale faithfulness primarily through evidence perturbation rather than surface plausibility.","delta_genome":"Compared with existing multimodal benchmarks, the key delta is to make grounding a first-class target. Each task has paired interventions: remove or blur the cited image region, mute the cited audio interval, mask the cited text span, or shuffle the cited video segment. A model passes only if confidence drops or the answer changes appropriately when its claimed evidence is ablated, and remains stable when irrelevant evidence is perturbed.","claim_genome":"A useful failure taxonomy should separate: perception failures, localization failures, temporal grounding failures, cross-modal binding failures, modality dominance or neglect, contradiction-resolution failures, causal-reasoning failures, hallucinated evidence, spurious-prior shortcuts, long-context retrieval failures, and unfaithful rationale failures. The benchmark claim is that evaluating answer plus evidence plus intervention sensitivity will better measure grounded multimodal reasoning than answer accuracy alone across text, image, audio, and video."}}
```

[result]
Additional taxonomy sources:
- Object hallucination: nonexistent object mentions.
- Attribute hallucination: wrong color, size, count, identity.
- Relation hallucination: incorrect spatial or social relation.
- Temporal hallucination: wrong event order or duration.
- Audio hallucination: inferring unseen/inaudible source incorrectly.
- Causal hallucination: claiming cause-effect from correlation.
- Modality dominance: one modality overrides contradictory evidence from another.

full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for investigating transparent data provenance systems in scientific machine learning workflows.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll first identify core systems/papers spanning ML experiment tracking, provenance capture, and collaborative data-analysis lifecycle management, then use the gaps to shape a concrete investigation plan.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "transparent data provenance machine learning lifecycle scientific workflows W3C PROV MLflow noWorkflow ProvDB",
  "discipline": "computer_science",
  "k": 5
}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include `arXiv:1907.04971` — “MLflow: A Machine Learning Lifecycle Platform”; `DOI:10.1145/2882903.2915240` — “ProvDB: Provenance-Enabled Lifecycle Management of Collaborative Data Analysis Workflows”; and `DOI:10.1007/978-3-319-16462-5_7` — “noWorkflow: Capturing and Analyzing Provenance of Scripts.”

Rationale: MLflow is a central baseline because it is widely used for experiment tracking in ML workflows, but its provenance model is typically run-centric rather than full data-lineage-centric.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "arXiv:1907.04971"
}
```
[result]: MLflow records parameters, metrics, artifacts, models, and project environments to support reproducibility across the ML lifecycle. However, it does not natively provide fine-grained, transparent provenance over dataset versions, preprocessing transformations, array-level scientific data products, or tamper-evident lineage graphs.

Rationale: I’ll compare MLflow with provenance-native systems that capture script execution and collaborative workflow evolution, because scientific ML often lives between ad hoc Python scripts and managed workflow systems.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "DOI:10.1007/978-3-319-16462-5_7"
}
```
[result]: noWorkflow captures provenance from script executions without requiring users to adopt a workflow engine, making it relevant for exploratory scientific Python workflows. Its strength is transparent capture of execution provenance, but it is not specifically designed around modern ML lifecycle artifacts, large scientific arrays, dataset hashes, or model-registry integration.

Rationale: I’ll check novelty by positioning the idea against MLflow-style tracking, noWorkflow-style script provenance, and ProvDB-style collaborative lifecycle management.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "paper_ids": [
    "arXiv:1907.04971",
    "DOI:10.1007/978-3-319-16462-5_7",
    "DOI:10.1145/2882903.2915240"
  ],
  "candidate_idea": "A transparent provenance layer for scientific machine learning workflows that combines ML experiment tracking, W3C PROV-compatible lineage graphs, content-addressed scientific datasets, and replayable provenance verification across preprocessing, training, evaluation, and publication artifacts."
}

These papers consistently frame sub-solar mergers as a primordial-black-hole target requiring expanded low-mass template banks and careful high-frequency/noise treatment.  

The O2 LIGO paper is a baseline for how existing detectors were adapted: it documents template-bank mass ranges, duration/computing issues, and astrophysical/PBH interpretation.

No significant candidates were found; the work set merger-rate upper limits and PBH dark-matter-fraction constraints, while noting the long waveform durations and large template banks as key practical costs.  

The O3a paper updates the state of the art and is useful for identifying what detector/pipeline improvements mattered with later sensitivity and Virgo participation.

Commun. 7, 12950, DOI:10.1038/ncomms12950. The candidates connect QEC-enhanced sensing theory with a concrete trapped-ion interferometry platform.

I’ll read the QEC-metrology theory first to identify the condition under which error correction helps rather than removes the signal.

[result]: Kessler et al. show that QEC can restore Heisenberg-like scaling or extend coherence in metrology if the noise operators are correctable while the signal Hamiltonian has a component outside the Lindblad/noise span. The key design principle is to encode the sensor so dominant noise is syndrome-detectable but the parameter-dependent phase remains logical.

Next I’ll read the trapped-ion interferometry paper to ground the experimental proposal in realistic observables and noise sources.

Reported limitations include motional heating, laser phase/intensity noise, magnetic-field noise, and contrast loss during interferometer time.

[result]: Found core candidates: Chan et al., “Quantum Mechanical Actuation of Microelectromechanical Systems by the Casimir Force,” Science 291, 1941–1944 (2001), DOI: 10.1126/science.1057984; Decca et al., “Measurement of the Casimir Force between Dissimilar Metals,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 91, 050402 (2003), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.91.050402; Chan et al., “Measurement of the Casimir Force between a Gold Sphere and a Silicon Surface with Nanoscale Trench Arrays,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 101, 030401 (2008), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.101.030401; Chen et al., “Demonstration of the Lateral Casimir Force,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 101801 (2002), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.88.101801.
The MEMS papers establish force-gradient readout, pull-in limits, calibration, and noise constraints that should define the measurement architecture.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1126/science.1057984","10.1103/PhysRevLett.91.050402"]}
```  
[result]: Chan et al. showed Casimir attraction can actuate a torsional MEMS oscillator and cause nonlinear pull-in, using sphere–plate geometry to avoid parallelism sensitivity. Decca et al. used a micromachined torsional oscillator with electrostatic calibration and dynamic response to measure Casimir forces precisely at submicron separations.
The complex-geometry papers identify what must be extended: patterned surfaces introduce lateral/shape dependence, but the metrology must separate geometry effects from residual electrostatics and roughness.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1103/PhysRevLett.101.030401","10.1103/PhysRevLett.88.101801"]}
```  
[result]: Chan et al. measured normal Casimir forces for a gold sphere above nanoscale silicon trench arrays and found deviations from simple proximity-force predictions. Chen et al. demonstrated a lateral Casimir force between sinusoidally corrugated surfaces, proving that lateral phase control can expose geometry-dependent vacuum forces.
A concise gap check helps make the pathway more than a repeat of sphere–plate or single-grating measurements.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"A MEMS torsional/frequency-shift platform with interchangeable patterned chips to map Casimir force gradients across separation, lateral phase, rotation angle, and geometry class, benchmarking scattering-theory predictions for non-PFA complex geometries."}
```

full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to examine how entanglement entropy evolves during thermalization in isolated many-body quantum systems.
chosen:
rationale: I first search for anchor papers that directly connect post-quench entanglement growth, ETH/thermalization, and isolated many-body experiments/simulations, so the plan is grounded in established mechanisms rather than generic thermalization language.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"entanglement entropy evolution thermalization isolated many-body quantum systems quench ETH arXiv","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include Calabrese & Cardy, “Evolution of entanglement entropy in one-dimensional systems” (arXiv:cond-mat/0503393); Kim & Huse, “Ballistic spreading of entanglement in a diffusive nonintegrable system” (arXiv:1306.4306); and Kaufman et al., “Quantum thermalization through entanglement in an isolated many-body system” (arXiv:1603.04409). These cover quasiparticle theory, numerical nonintegrable dynamics, and experimental observation.

rationale: I read the theory papers to extract the core expected time dependence: linear entanglement growth after a global quench, saturation to a volume-law value, and differences between integrable and nonintegrable systems.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306"],"focus":"post-quench entanglement entropy growth laws, saturation, integrability vs nonintegrability, relation to thermal entropy"}
```
[result]: Calabrese & Cardy derive a quasiparticle picture in which entanglement entropy of a subsystem grows approximately linearly until a light-cone time set by subsystem size, then saturates. Kim & Huse show in a nonintegrable spin chain that entanglement spreads ballistically even when conserved quantities diffuse, and the long-time entanglement entropy approaches the thermal entropy density.

rationale: I then read the experimental paper to make the plan measurable, emphasizing what can be compared to subsystem thermalization rather than only wavefunction-level simulation.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:1603.04409"],"focus":"experimental measurement of entanglement entropy and thermalization in isolated many-body systems"}
```
[result]: Kaufman et al. measured second Rényi entanglement entropy in isolated bosonic atoms and showed that global pure-state dynamics can generate local thermal behavior via entanglement between subsystems. The paper motivates tracking subsystem entropy growth, particle-number entropy, and comparison of reduced density matrices to thermal predictions.

rationale: Before proposing the study, I check whether the contribution is a mere reproduction or can be framed as a useful comparative protocol across thermalizing, integrable, and localized regimes with common diagnostics.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306","arXiv:1603.04409"],"proposed_idea":"Benchmark entanglement-entropy evolution during isolated-system thermalization across nonintegrable, integrable, and many-body-localized spin chains using the same quench protocol, comparing von Neumann/Rényi entropies, entanglement spectrum, local observables, ETH/GGE predictions, and saturation to thermodynamic entropy."}
```
[result]: The individual ingredients are established, but a unified benchmarking protocol that directly contrasts entanglement growth, subsystem thermalization quality, and entropy-density saturation across ETH, GGE, and MBL regimes is a coherent and publishable computational study if implemented with careful finite-size and bond-dimension controls. Novelty is moderate: strongest if the work identifies quantitative crossover diagnostics rather than only reproducing linear/logarithmic growth laws.

rationale: I now propose an idea_plan that uses these retrieved results as the baseline: quasiparticle linear growth, ballistic entanglement spreading, saturation near thermal entropy in ETH systems, and measurable subsystem Rényi/von Neumann entropies.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","grounding_paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306","arXiv:1603.04409"]}
```

- Entanglement entropy of a subsystem grows approximately linearly until quasiparticles traverse the subsystem.
- Saturation occurs at a value proportional to subsystem size.
- Strong applicability to integrable or conformal settings; less direct for generic chaotic models.

- Isolated nonintegrable systems thermalize locally under unitary dynamics.
- ETH concerns local observables more directly than entanglement entropy, but entanglement of eigenstates is generally volume-law and close to thermal entropy density for subsystems.

- Local observables retain memory of initial conditions.
- Thermalization is obstructed despite interactions.
- Entanglement growth provides a diagnostic separating ergodic and localized dynamics.

chosen:

```action

```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Lelli et al. “SPARC: Mass Models for 175 Disk Galaxies…” arXiv:1606.09251; McGaugh et al. “Radial Acceleration Relation…” arXiv:1609.05917; Bekenstein “Relativistic gravitation theory for the MOND paradigm” arXiv:astro-ph/0403694; Skordis & Zlosnik “New Relativistic Theory for Modified Newtonian Dynamics” arXiv:2007.00082; Saffari & Rahvar “f(R) gravity: From the Pioneer anomaly to the cosmic acceleration” arXiv:0708.1482; and Brout et al. “The Pantheon+ Analysis” arXiv:2202.04077.  

SPARC/RAR define the empirical galaxy-side target: any proposed gravity law must reproduce detailed rotation-curve phenomenology, not just asymptotic flatness.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1606.09251"}
```  
[result]: Lelli et al. present SPARC, 175 disk galaxies with high-quality rotation curves and 3.6 μm photometry, enabling baryonic mass modeling. This dataset is widely used to test MOND-like laws and halo models across surface brightness and morphology.  

To connect galactic modified dynamics with a relativistic/cosmological framework, I’ll read a modern MOND-motivated theory rather than only phenomenological rotation-curve fits.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2007.00082"}
```  
[result]: Skordis & Zlosnik construct a relativistic MOND theory designed to recover MOND behavior in galaxies while being compatible with relativistic cosmology. The work motivates testing whether a single covariant model can satisfy both weak-field galactic constraints and homogeneous-background cosmological observations.  

Now I’ll check whether the proposed angle—joint inference on rotation curves and expansion history with shared modified-gravity parameters—goes beyond existing single-domain or loosely connected tests.

[result]: Found candidates including Teufel et al. “Sideband cooling of micromechanical motion to the quantum ground state” (Nature 2011, paper_id: doi:10.1038/nature10261), Chan et al. “Laser cooling of a nanomechanical oscillator into its quantum ground state” (Nature 2011, paper_id: doi:10.1038/nature10461), Marinković et al. “Optomechanical Bell Test” (PRL 2018, paper_id: doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.220404), and Bahrami et al. “Noninterferometric test of collapse models in optomechanical systems” (PRL 2014, paper_id: doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.210404).
The first read checks the experimental baseline: what levels of cooling and coherent measurement have actually been achieved in mechanical resonators. These set realistic starting points for any boundary experiment.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1038/nature10261","doi:10.1038/nature10461"],"fields":["system","method","key_results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Teufel et al. used a microwave electromechanical circuit to sideband-cool a micromechanical oscillator to near its motional ground state with mean phonon occupancy below one. Chan et al. achieved optical sideband cooling of a nanobeam optomechanical crystal to ground-state occupation. Both establish quantum-compatible preparation, but neither directly creates large spatial superpositions or tests deviations from standard decoherence.
The second read targets the actual boundary question: whether mechanics can show nonclassical correlations and whether excess diffusion/collapse mechanisms can be constrained without full spatial interferometry.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.220404","doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.210404"],"fields":["system","method","key_results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Marinković et al. proposed/implemented an optomechanical Bell-test architecture using photon-phonon pair creation/readout to reveal nonclassical light-mechanics correlations, but loopholes and small effective masses limit macroscopicity. Bahrami et al. proposed using unexplained mechanical heating/diffusion as a noninterferometric test of collapse models, with sensitivity improving for massive, cold, low-noise resonators.
A useful experiment should combine both directions—Bell/nonclassicality witness and collapse/decoherence scaling—rather than repeating ground-state cooling alone. I’ll check novelty around a protocol that scans mass, superposition separation, and environmental decoherence in the same device family.

full_prompt: Propose a study on self-healing concrete incorporating microcapsules, bacteria, or mineral admixtures to extend infrastructure lifetime.
chosen:
I will anchor the proposal in three established self-healing routes—bacterial CaCO₃ precipitation, encapsulated polymer/mineral agents, and crystalline/mineral admixtures—then identify a gap around hybridization, crack-width targeting, and lifetime-relevant durability tests.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"self-healing concrete bacteria microcapsules crystalline admixture crack closure durability infrastructure lifetime key papers DOI","discipline":"materials","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidates included Jonkers et al. 2010, *Ecological Engineering*, DOI: 10.1016/j.ecoleng.2008.12.036; Van Tittelboom et al. 2010, *Cement and Concrete Research*, DOI: 10.1016/j.cemconres.2009.08.025; Roig-Flores et al. 2015, *Construction and Building Materials*, DOI: 10.1016/j.conbuildmat.2015.03.091; and microcapsule-focused cementitious self-healing studies/reviews noting polymeric shell rupture and healing-agent release.
Bacterial self-healing is a mature route but often limited by bacterial survival, nutrient availability, and crack-width dependence; I’ll read a foundational study to ground the mechanism and constraints.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.ecoleng.2008.12.036"}
```  
[result]: Jonkers et al. describe alkali-resistant bacteria embedded with a calcium lactate-based nutrient to precipitate CaCO₃ inside cracks, improving crack sealing and water tightness. The paper highlights viability loss in harsh cement matrices and the need for protective carriers or reservoirs for long-term function.
Mineral/crystalline admixtures are attractive for infrastructure because they are inexpensive and compatible with ready-mix practice; I’ll read a durability-oriented study to capture environmental dependencies.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.conbuildmat.2015.03.091"}
```  
[result]: Roig-Flores et al. show that crystalline admixtures can enhance autogenous crack sealing, particularly when water exposure enables continued hydration and crystal growth. Performance depends strongly on curing/exposure regime and crack size, with incomplete recovery under less favorable environments.
To convert the evidence into design variables, I’ll extract a genome summarizing mechanisms, niches, observations, and limitations across bacterial, microcapsule, and mineral-admixture approaches.  
```action
